title: Hot Cross Bun Sign
body:
→ Definition: Cruciform T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the pons
→ Location: Central pons (basis pontis)
→ Pathology: Degeneration of pontocerebellar fibers + gliosis
→ Classically seen in: Multiple system atrophy – cerebellar type (MSA-C)
→ Associated findings: Cerebellar atrophy, MCP hyperintensity, pontine atrophy
→ Differentials: Spinocerebellar ataxia, chronic ischemia, demyelination
→ Key clue: **Symmetrical, well-defined cross pattern favors neurodegeneration**

title: Pulvinar Sign(CJD)
body:
→ Imaging: FLAIR/DWI → bilateral pulvinar hyperintensity
→ Classic for: Variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD)
→ Extension: May involve dorsomedial thalamus → “hockey stick sign”
→ Differentials (critical):
Japanese encephalitis (India relevance)
Deep venous thrombosis
Wernicke encephalopathy
→ Sequence sensitivity: **DWI most sensitive early**
→ Clinical context mandatory: **Rapid dementia, psychiatric features**
→ Pearl: Symmetry + clinical setting key

title: Panda Sign (Wilson Disease)
body:
→ Location: Midbrain
→ Appearance: Preserved red nuclei + hypointense substantia nigra + hyperintense tegmentum → “panda face”
→ Disease: Wilson disease (copper deposition)
→ Other findings: Basal ganglia T2 hyperintensity, atrophy
→ Differentials:
Leigh disease
Hypoxic injury
→ Clinical correlation: Hepatic dysfunction + neuropsychiatric symptoms
→ Pearl: Not always present → supportive, not diagnostic

title: Molar Tooth Sign (Joubert Syndrome)
body:
→  Components (must identify all): 
Thickened, elongated superior cerebellar peduncles, 
deep interpeduncular fossa, 
vermian hypoplasia.
→ Appearance: The axial brainstem resembles a molar tooth.
→ Category: Hindbrain malformation.
→ Associated anomalies: renal and retinal abnormalities.
→ Clinical: hypotonia and developmental delay.
→ Pitfall: Vermian hypoplasia alone does not equal Joubert.

title: Eye of the Tiger Sign (NBIA- Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation)
body:
→ Location: Globus pallidus
→ Appearance: Central T2 hyperintensity with surrounding hypointense rim
→ Pathology: Iron deposition + gliosis
→ Classic for: PKAN (NBIA spectrum)
→ Differentials:
Atypical NBIA variants
Wilson disease (less classic pattern)
→ Clinical context: Movement disorders, dystonia
→ Pearl: Highly suggestive but not 100% specific

title: White Cerebellum Sign
body:
→ Appearance: Cerebellum appears relatively hyperdense 
→ Mechanism: Preferential supratentorial edema with relative infratentorial preservation
→ Seen in: Severe hypoxic injury, trauma
→ Important distinction: Not true cerebellar hyperdensity—relative effect
→ Associated: Diffuse cerebral edema, loss of gray-white differentiation
→ Prognosis: Poor, similar to reversal sign
→ Pitfall: Windowing or beam hardening artifacts

title: Reversal Sign (Severe Hypoxic-Ischemic Injury)
body:
→  Imaging: CT reversal of normal gray-white attenuation (white matter appears relatively hyperdense)
→ Mechanism: Diffuse cerebral edema → gray matter density drops more than white
→ Often seen in: Post-cardiac arrest, severe hypoxia (pediatric > adult)
→ Associated findings: Effaced sulci, small ventricles, cerebellar sparing
→ Differential: Pseudo-reversal (technical factors, windowing)
→ Prognosis: Very poor → indicates severe global injury
→ Pearl: Always correlate clinically; not all cases show classic reversal

title: Dural Tail Sign (Extra-axial Lesion Clue)
body:
→ Definition: Enhancing dural thickening tapering away from lesion
→ Mechanism: Combination of tumor infiltration + reactive hyperemia(mostly).
→ Sequences: Best seen on post-contrast T1
→ Associated extra-axial signs:
CSF cleft
Buckling of gray matter
Broad dural base
→ Differentials with dural tail:
Meningioma (most common)
Gbm
Lymphoma
PXA
Metastasis
Granulomatous disease (TB in Indian context—important)
→ Pitfall: Not pathognomonic of meningioma

title: Ivy Sign (Leptomeningeal Collaterals)
body:
→ Sequences:
FLAIR: sulcal hyperintensity
Post-contrast T1: leptomeningeal enhancement
→ Mechanism: Slow flow + engorged pial vessels -incomplete CSF suppression
→ Distribution: Diffuse or focal, often watershed regions
→ Seen in:
Moyamoya (classic)
Chronic ICA stenosis/occlusion
→ Correlation:
Matches hypoperfusion on perfusion imaging
Indicates poor cerebrovascular reserve
→ Dynamic nature: Decreases after revascularization surgery
→ Pitfalls:
Meningitis (clinical context critical)

title: Diffuse Pontine glioma
body:
→ These tumors typically present in childhood (3 to 10 years of age).
→ There is an association with neurofibromatosis type I.
→ The basilar artery displaced anteriorly against the clivus and potentially engulfed. 
→ Occasionally the tumor is exophytic, either outwards into the basal cisterns or centrally in the 4th ventricle.
→ DTI: disruption of the white matter tracts indicates diffuse midline glioma rather than focal brainstem gliomas.
→ Radiotherapy is the mainstay of treatment.

imgs.label: Diffuse Pontine glioma engulfing basilar artery

title: Hypertrophic olivary degeneration
body:
→ Clinical - Palatal myoclonus - maynnot be seen in all cases.
→Main role of mri is not to be confused with other lesions
→ It is a trans-synaptic degeneration due to interruption to traingle of Gillian and Mollaret
→ Can be bilateral

title: Points to remember - Posterior fossa lesions
body:
→ Try to mention the amount of extension of tumor into IAC in schwanomas and also comment on dominance of venous sinuses  as they may be compromised during surgery 
→ Be wary of pulsation artefacts in determining the exact lesion extent 
→ Ependymomas are plastic tumors - They extend into foramen of magendi/lushka
→ Cyst is a component of tumor in pilocytic astrocytoma where cyst wall will enhance and it needs to be resected. However, in hemangioblastoma, cyst is mostly a peritumoral cyst and it need not be resected. 
Cyst wall will not enhance. Prominent flowvoids helps is the diagnosis 
→ Sometimes it will be difficult to differentiate intra axial vs extraaxial in posterior fossa especially if the lesion is located peripherally within the cerebellum. 
→ Nodular variant of medullobalstoma will mimic Lehrimitte duclos syndrome
→ Pontine infarcts donot cross midline since they are supplied by perforators however if basilar artery is involved they may cross.

title: Pineal region anatomy
body:
→ Pineal region lesions can cause perinaud syndrome if they compress on tectal plate( superior colliculus- upward gaze palsy).
→ Assessing internal cerebral veins helps in distinguishing if tumor is arising from pineal gland or adjacent structures.

title: Anterior temporal lobe perivascular space
body:
→ Anterior temporal lobe perivascular spaces **often have surrounding edema** (~80%) which can range from mild to extensive .
→  Additionally, **they are usually associated with contacting a branch of the middle cerebral artery **(~90%) .
→ Otherwise, they have similar appearances to perivascular spaces elsewhere: internal CSF signal on all sequences and no enhancement . 
→ Can be confused with cystic tumor especially DNET/Ganglioglioma 
→ **Reactive astrocytosis can be mistaken for a glial tumor on histopathology leading to further surgery and treatment, especially as the tissue will naturally enough be IDH wildtype.**

imgs.label: Note the close relationship of cyst with MCA and adjacent edema

title: Types of perivascular spaces
body:
→ Type 1: located in the area supplied by the lenticulostriate arteries entering the basal ganglia

→ Type 2: located in the area supplied by the perforating medullary arteries as they enter the cortical grey matter

→ Type 3: located in the midbrain

→ Type 4: temporal pole, insular

title: Central vein sign- Multiple sclerosis
body: → Seen on high tesla(3T or more) on SWI in multiple sclerosis.

title: Hummingbird Sign (PSP)
body:
→ Appearance: Midbrain atrophy on midline sagittal T1 resembles a hummingbird — small midbrain body with preserved pons (the "beak")
→ Measurement: Midbrain AP diameter < 14 mm; Midbrain:Pons ratio < 0.52
→ Classic for: Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP)
→ Axial equivalent: Mickey Mouse / Morning Glory sign (see separate note)
→ Mechanism: Selective midbrain tegmentum neurodegeneration
→ Associated findings: Dilated third ventricle, superior cerebellar peduncle atrophy, frontal lobe atrophy
→ Differentials: MSA-P (pons atrophies more), CBD, PD (midbrain relatively preserved)
→ Key clue: Pons is normal sized — if pons also small, think MSA not PSP
→ Sensitivity/Specificity: High specificity (~99.5%) but low sensitivity (~50%) — absent sign does not exclude PSP; MRPI (MR Parkinsonism Index) has higher diagnostic accuracy
→ Clinical: Vertical gaze palsy, falls, axial rigidity, dementia

title: Mickey Mouse Sign (PSP)
body:
→ Appearance: Axial T1/T2 at midbrain level — concave lateral margins of midbrain tegmentum
→ Mickey Mouse sign and Morning Glory sign are closely related axial signs, often used interchangeably:
  Both describe concave lateral margins of the midbrain tegmentum on axial imaging
  Morning Glory : specifically the lateral concavity resembling the morning glory flower
  Mickey Mouse: same lateral concavity + reduced AP diameter giving Mickey Mouse ear shape
→ Both are axial equivalents of the Hummingbird sign (sagittal) — all seen in PSP
→ Midbrain cross-sectional area < 100 mm² significant (Quattrone criteria)
→ Classic for: PSP
→ Associated: Superior cerebellar peduncle thinning (best on coronal)
→ Pitfall: Do not confuse with normal interpeduncular fossa widening in elderly
→ Clinical: Vertical gaze palsy + postural instability

title: Dawson's Fingers (MS)
body:
→ Appearance: Periventricular T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions oriented perpendicular to lateral ventricles
→ Best seen on: Sagittal FLAIR — finger-like projections along medullary veins
→ Mechanism: Demyelination tracks along perivenular inflammation (inside-out model)
→ Classic for: Multiple Sclerosis
→ Location criteria (McDonald): Juxtacortical, periventricular, infratentorial, spinal cord
→ Differentials: NMOSD (confluent, not finger-like), MOGAD, vasculitis, migraine (non-perpendicular)
→ Key clue: Perpendicular orientation to ventricles is the distinguishing feature
→ Associated: Central vein sign on SWI (see separate note)
→ Pitfall: Small vessel ischemic disease can mimic — but not perpendicular

title: Butterfly / Batwing Sign (GBM)
body:
→ Appearance: Heterogeneous enhancing mass crossing corpus callosum symmetrically — axial shape resembles butterfly wings
→ Classic for: Glioblastoma (GBM) — most common cause of corpus callosum crossing mass
→ Mechanism: Tumor spreads via white matter tracts — corpus callosum is a direct highway
→ Imaging: Irregular thick enhancing ring, central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic edema, restricted DWI at rim
→ Differentials of corpus callosum crossing lesion:
  Primary CNS Lymphoma (homogeneous enhancement, periventricular, restricted DWI throughout)
  Tumefactive MS (open ring, less mass effect)
  Marchiafava-Bignami (DWI restriction, no mass, alcohol history)
→ Key clue: Irregular necrotic ring + crossing = GBM until proven otherwise
→ Pitfall: Lymphoma can cross too — look for homogeneous enhancement and DWI restriction throughout

title: Knife Blade Atrophy (FTD)
body:
→ Appearance: Severe focal cortical atrophy of frontal and/or temporal lobes — gyri appear thin and blade-like
→ Classic for: Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) — especially semantic variant PPA and Pick's disease
→ Distribution:
  Frontotemporal variant — bilateral frontal > temporal
  Semantic variant PPA — asymmetric anterior temporal (left > right)
  Nonfluent variant — left perisylvian
→ Imaging: Dilated Sylvian fissure and frontal horns, thinned gyri, spared posterior cortex
→ Differentials: Alzheimer's (posterior > anterior, hippocampal atrophy), PSP (midbrain), CBS (asymmetric parietal)
→ Key clue: Posterior cortex and hippocampi relatively spared in FTD (opposite of AD)
→ Clinical: Behavioural change / aphasia in relatively younger patient (< 65 years)

title: Tram Track Sign (Sturge-Weber)
body:
→ Appearance: Parallel curvilinear cortical calcifications following gyral contours — like tramlines
→ Modality: CT best for calcification; MRI shows cortical atrophy + leptomeningeal enhancement
→ Classic for: Sturge-Weber Syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis)
→ Mechanism: Leptomeningeal venous angioma → chronic ischemia → dystrophic calcification
→ Distribution: Usually unilateral posterior (parieto-occipital), ipsilateral to facial port-wine stain
→ Associated: Ipsilateral cerebral atrophy, choroid plexus enlargement, deep venous enlargement
→ Contrast: Leptomeningeal enhancement in neonates before calcification develops
→ Differentials: Bilateral calcification — think Fahr disease, TORCH, metabolic
→ Clinical: Facial port-wine stain (V1/V2), seizures, contralateral hemiparesis, glaucoma

title: Empty Delta Sign (Sinus Thrombosis)
body:
→ Appearance: Triangular filling defect in posterior superior sagittal sinus on contrast CT — enhancing dural walls with non-enhancing clot inside
→ Modality: Contrast CT (classic); MRI/MRV superior for diagnosis
→ Classic for: Superior Sagittal Sinus Thrombosis (CVST)
→ Seen in: Minority of CVST cases (~10–30%) — not sensitive, absence does not exclude
→ MRI equivalent: T1 hyperintense clot in sinus (subacute), loss of flow void, MRV filling defect
→ Differentials: Arachnoid granulation (normal variant — eccentric, CSF signal), high-riding jugular
→ Associated findings: Bilateral parasagittal venous infarcts (haemorrhagic), sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity
→ Key clue: Bilateral parasagittal haemorrhagic infarcts should always raise CVST
→ Indian context: Dehydration, postpartum, OCP use, prothrombotic states

title: Target Sign (Tuberculoma)
body:
→ Appearance: Central nidus of calcification or central dot of enhancement surrounded by a ring of enhancement
→ Classic for: CNS Tuberculoma — historically considered pathognomonic
→ Pathology: Central calcific nidus (or granulomatous core) + surrounding granulomatous rim
→ Key MRI features:
  Thick T2 hypointense rim (paramagnetic free radicals in caseous necrosis)
  Central calcification or enhancing nidus
  T1 iso/hypointense center
  Surrounding vasogenic edema
  No central DWI restriction (unlike pyogenic abscess)
→ MRS: Lipid-lactate peak (caseous necrosis); no choline elevation (unlike tumor)
→ Do not confuse with:
  Eccentric target sign (Toxoplasmosis — post-contrast T1, eccentric mural nodule, 3-zone pattern)
  Concentric target sign on T2 (also Toxoplasmosis — alternating concentric rings)
→ Differentials of ring-enhancing lesion:
  Pyogenic abscess (thin smooth ring, central DWI restriction, dual rim on SWI)
  Metastasis (grey-white junction, known primary, thinner rim)
  Toxoplasmosis (HIV, multiple, basal ganglia, eccentric target)
→ Indian context: TB endemic — always in differential for ring-enhancing lesion, even immunocompetent
→ Associated: Leptomeningeal enhancement (basal cisterns), hydrocephalus, infarcts (TB vasculitis)
→ Pitfall: Treated/calcified tuberculoma may not enhance — do not dismiss as incidental
→ Key clue: Central calcific dot + ring enhancement = TB target sign

title: Dual Rim Sign (Pyogenic Abscess)
body:
→ Appearance: Two concentric rims surrounding the lesion cavity on T2/FLAIR/SWI
→ Outer rim: T2 hypointense (low signal) — paramagnetic free radical production by macrophages in capsule
→ Inner rim: T2 hyperintense (high signal) — corresponds to enhancing capsule on T1+C, represents granulation tissue
→ Classic for: Pyogenic brain abscess — seen in ~75% of cerebral abscesses (Radiopaedia)
→ Also described in: Tuberculoma — dual rim can be seen; does not exclude TB
→ SWI/GRE: Outer hypointense rim blooms — increases conspicuity
→ Complements: Central DWI restriction (pus restricts) + thin ring on T1+C
→ Most specific imaging feature: Dual rim = most specific MRI sign of pyogenic abscess
→ Differentials:
  GBM (thick irregular ring, no dual rim, DWI at rim only)
  Tuberculoma (dual rim possible, thick T2 dark rim, target sign, no central DWI)
  Tumefactive demyelination (open ring toward cortex, no dual rim)
→ Key clue: Central DWI restriction + dual rim = abscess; absence of dual rim does not exclude abscess
→ Indian context: Dental/otogenic/cardiac source; cyanotic heart disease in children

title: Blooming Artifact (SWI/GRE)
body:
→ Definition: Signal loss larger than the actual lesion size on T2*/GRE/SWI — due to magnetic susceptibility
→ Causes of blooming:
  Hemosiderin (old blood) — cavernoma, DAI, hypertensive microbleeds
  Calcification — can bloom on GRE (less than blood)
  Air/bone interface
  Iron deposition — NBIA, basal ganglia in neurodegeneration
  Flow artifact — vessels
→ Classic use: Cavernous malformation — popcorn morphology with hemosiderin ring
→ SWI vs GRE: SWI more sensitive for microbleeds and venous structures
→ Key clue: If a lesion seen on GRE/SWI but not on T2 — think susceptibility artifact, not true lesion
→ Differentials of multiple microbleeds: CAA / cerebral amyloid angiopathy (lobar > deep), hypertensive (deep > lobar), DAI (grey-white junction + corpus callosum)
→ Pitfall: Calcification also blooms — CT needed to differentiate blood from calcium

title: Swirl Sign (Hyperacute SDH)
body:
→ Definition: Non-contrast CT appearance of acute extravasation of blood into a haematoma — unclotted fresh blood (lower attenuation) within clotted blood (higher attenuation)
→ Applies to: ANY intracranial haematoma — intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH), subdural haematoma, extradural haematoma
→ Significance: Predictor of haematoma expansion — indicates active/ongoing bleeding
→ Mechanism: Unclotted blood = coagulation cascade not yet complete → lower HU than surrounding clot
→ Related sign: Black hole sign (ICH) = encapsulated swirl sign, well-defined hypodense region ≥28 HU less than surrounding haematoma
→ CTA corollary: Spot sign = same phenomenon but extravasated contrast on CTA
→ In SDH specifically: Swirl sign = mixed density collection, indicates active rebleeding or coagulopathy
→ Clinical significance: Associated with poor prognosis and need for urgent intervention
→ Key clue: Hypodense area within hyperdense haematoma on NCCT = active bleeding until proven otherwise

title: Empty Sella Sign
body:
→ Appearance: Pituitary fossa appears filled with CSF — flat pituitary gland along floor, infundibulum centrally positioned
→ Primary empty sella: Herniation of subarachnoid space through incomplete diaphragma sellae — arachnoid CSF fills sella
→ Secondary empty sella: Post-treatment (surgery, radiotherapy, infarction of pituitary)
→ Associations: Raised ICP, obesity, CSF leak, IIH (idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
→ Imaging clue: Infundibulum reaches pituitary floor centrally — distinguishes from cystic lesion (where infundibulum displaced)
→ Differentials of cystic sellar lesion:
  Rathke cleft cyst (intrasellar, variable T1 signal — often bright but can be dark/intermediate, no infundibulum sign)
  Craniopharyngioma (suprasellar, calcification, mixed signal)
  Pituitary adenoma with cystic change
→ Key clue: Infundibulum central + CSF signal throughout = empty sella (not a cyst)
→ Clinical: Usually incidental; assess for IIH features (papilledema, visual field defect)

title: Sunburst / Spoke Wheel Pattern (Meningioma)
body:
→ Appearance: Radially arranged vessels from tumor center on angiography — resembling sunburst or spoke-wheel
→ Modality: DSA (classic), CTA can show feeding vessels
→ Classic for: Meningioma (dural-based, external carotid supply via middle meningeal artery)
→ CT/MRI equivalent: Densely homogeneous enhancement, calcification, dural tail, CSF cleft
→ Vascular supply: External carotid (dural feeders) + internal carotid (pial feeders) — dual supply in large tumors
→ Differentials of dural-based enhancing mass: Solitary fibrous tumor / SFT (formerly hemangiopericytoma — WHO 2021), dural metastasis, lymphoma, TB granuloma
→ Key clue: Dural base + homogeneous enhancement + calcification + dural tail = meningioma
→ Pitfall: Dural tail is NOT specific for meningioma
→ Grading: Most WHO grade 1 but grade 2/3 show invasion, necrosis, irregular margins

title: Salt and Pepper Pattern (Paraganglioma)
body:
→ Appearance: Heterogeneous signal on T1/T2 — multiple punctate flow voids (pepper) within hyperintense stroma (salt)
→ Classic for: Paraganglioma / Glomus tumor (glomus jugulare, glomus tympanicum, carotid body)
→ Mechanism: Hypervascularity — multiple enlarged feeding vessels appear as flow voids
→ Location: Jugular foramen (glomus jugulare), middle ear (glomus tympanicum), carotid bifurcation
→ CT: Permeative moth-eaten destruction of jugular foramen bone (glomus jugulare)
→ Enhancement: Intense, avid — highly vascular tumor
→ Differentials at jugular foramen: Schwannoma (smooth remodeling, no bony destruction), meningioma (hyperostosis), metastasis
→ Key clue: Salt and pepper + jugular foramen mass + bony destruction = glomus jugulare
→ Clinical: Pulsatile tinnitus, lower cranial nerve palsies, retrotympanic reddish mass
→ Nuclear medicine: DOTATATE PET highly sensitive for paragangliomas

title: CMV Encephalitis — MRI Pattern
body:
→ Note: 'Owl's eye' is a histological term — refers to CMV intranuclear inclusion bodies (Cowdry type A) on pathology, NOT an MRI sign
→ MRI appearance: Bilateral periventricular/subependymal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity + subependymal enhancement
→ Classic for: CMV (Cytomegalovirus) encephalitis — immunocompromised (HIV, transplant)
→ Mechanism: CMV infects ependymal cells → periventricular inflammation and necrosis
→ Imaging: Subependymal/ependymal enhancement, periventricular calcifications (congenital CMV), ventriculomegaly
→ Congenital CMV: Periventricular calcifications + cortical dysplasia + cerebellar hypoplasia
→ Differentials of periventricular enhancement:
  Primary CNS Lymphoma (ependymal spread)
  GBM (subependymal spread)
  Toxoplasmosis (less periventricular)
→ Indian context: Always consider CMV in HIV with periventricular lesions (common co-infection)
→ Key clue: Periventricular + immunocompromised + ependymal enhancement = CMV

title: Marchiafava-Bignami Disease  - Sandwich sign
body:
→ Appearance: DWI restriction and T2 hyperintensity in corpus callosum — body most commonly/initially involved, followed by genu and splenium; entire CC in severe cases
→ Sandwich sign: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in CENTRAL layers of CC with sparing of dorsal and ventral surfaces (layered necrosis of middle layer)
→ Classic for: Marchiafava-Bignami disease — demyelination/necrosis of corpus callosum
→ Setting: Chronic alcoholism (classically Italian red wine — historical, now recognized broadly)
→ Acute phase: DWI restriction, T2 hyperintensity, possible enhancement
→ Chronic phase: T2 hyperintensity, atrophy, cavitation of CC
→ Differentials of corpus callosum lesion:
  GBM (butterfly sign, irregular enhancement, mass effect)
  MS (ovoid, Dawson's fingers, other lesions)
  Lymphoma (dense enhancement, DWI restriction throughout)
  Ischemia (follows vascular territory)
→ Key clue: Alcoholic + corpus callosum DWI restriction + no mass = Marchiafava-Bignami
→ Clinical: Acute confusion, dysarthria, gait disturbance, dementia
→ Pitfall: Can look like MS — alcohol history is critical

title: Black Hole Sign (Chronic MS)
body:
→ Appearance: T1 hypointense lesions in white matter corresponding to T2 hyperintense MS plaques
→ Classic for: Chronic MS — indicates severe irreversible axonal loss and gliosis
→ Mechanism: Persistent T1 hypointensity = matrix destruction (axonal loss) not just edema
→ Transient black holes: T1 hypointensity in acute active lesion — may resolve with treatment
→ Permanent black holes: T1 hypointensity persisting > 6 months = irreversible damage
→ Significance: Correlates with physical disability and cognitive impairment better than T2 lesion load
→ Differentials: Normal perivascular spaces (follow CSF signal on all sequences), lacunar infarcts (vascular territory)
→ Key clue: T1 dark + corresponds to T2 plaque location = black hole (not CSF — doesn't follow ventricles)
→ Monitoring: Useful imaging biomarker for disease progression and treatment response

title: Cortical Ribbon Sign (CJD)
body:
→ Appearance: DWI hyperintensity along cortical ribbon — gyral pattern of restricted diffusion
→ Classic for: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (sCJD) — most common prion disease
→ Distribution: Asymmetric, multifocal — frontal, parietal, temporal cortex + basal ganglia + thalamus
→ Combination: Cortical ribboning + caudate/putamen DWI restriction = highly specific for sCJD
→ Pulvinar sign in vCJD (variant CJD — see separate note) vs cortical ribboning in sCJD
→ Sequence: DWI most sensitive and specific — abnormalities precede EEG/CSF changes
→ ADC: Profoundly reduced — amongst lowest ADC values in non-ischemic disease
→ Clinical: Rapidly progressive dementia (weeks-months), myoclonus, cerebellar ataxia, akinetic mutism
→ Differentials of rapid dementia + DWI changes:
  Autoimmune encephalitis (NMDAR, LGI1 — T2 hippocampal)
  Wernicke (mammillary bodies, periaqueductal)
  Paraneoplastic
→ Key clue: Rapidly progressive dementia + cortical DWI ribbon = CJD until proven otherwise

title: Hyperdense MCA Sign (Acute Stroke)
body:
→ Appearance: Hyperdense middle cerebral artery on non-contrast CT — indicates acute thrombus
→ Density: Thrombus > 60 HU (vs normal vessel ~40 HU)
→ Variants:
  Hyperdense MCA dot sign — clot in M2/M3 branch (Sylvian fissure)
  Hyperdense basilar artery — basilar artery occlusion (life-threatening)
→ Sensitivity: ~35-50% — not all clots are visible
→ Significance: Identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) — triage for thrombectomy
→ Associated early CT changes: Loss of insular ribbon, cortical sulcal effacement, grey-white blurring
→ ASPECTS scoring: Systematic 10-point CT assessment of MCA territory ischemia
→ Differentials of hyperdense vessel: Calcification (check bone windows), polycythemia (globally hyperdense)
→ Key clue: Unilateral hyperdense vessel + clinical stroke = LVO until proven otherwise
→ Next step: CTA immediately for vessel status

title: Fogging Phenomenon (Cerebral Infarct)
body:
→ Definition: Subacute cerebral infarct transiently regains near-normal density on CT — apparent normalisation
→ Correct term: 'Fogging phenomenon' (not 'fogging effect')
→ Mechanism: Migration of lipid-laden macrophages into infarcted cortex + capillary proliferation + resolution of oedema → density increases back toward normal
→ Timing: 2–3 weeks post infarct (6–36 days, median ~10 days); seen in approximately 50% of cases
→ Pitfall: CT at this stage can appear falsely normal — missed or underestimated infarct
→ Contrast CT during fogging: Gyriform cortical enhancement — confirms the infarct
→ MRI: Fogging also occurs on T2 (similar timing, similar mechanism) — DWI may begin pseudonormalising but T2/FLAIR still abnormal
→ Management: Never exclude infarct based on normal CT at 2 weeks — IV contrast CT or MRI to confirm
→ Key clue: Known stroke + normal-looking CT at 10–21 days = fogging phenomenon — get contrast CT or MRI

title: Lollipop Sign - LCH
body:
→ Appearance: Enhancing soft tissue nodule in hypothalamus/pituitary stalk with absent posterior pituitary bright spot — stalk thickening like a lollipop on coronal MRI
→ Classic for: Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis (LCH) involving CNS
→ CNS LCH targets: Hypothalamus, pituitary stalk, choroid plexus
→ CT skull: Calvarial lytic lesion with bevelled edge (inner table > outer table destruction) — geographic lysis
→ MRI: Enhancing stalk + absent posterior pituitary T1 bright spot (diabetes insipidus)
→ Differentials of pituitary stalk thickening:
  Germinoma (stalk + pineal — dual lesion, AFP/bHCG markers)
  Sarcoidosis (multisystem, hilar lymphadenopathy)
  Lymphoma
  Metastasis
→ Key clue: Child + diabetes insipidus + stalk thickening + calvarial lytic lesion = LCH
→ Clinical: Hand-Schuller-Christian triad = Diabetes insipidus + Exophthalmos + Lytic skull lesions (NOT growth failure — common exam error)

title: String of Beads Sign (Vasculitis / FMD)
body:
→ Appearance: Alternating segments of stenosis and dilation along vessel wall — beaded appearance
→ Classic for: Fibromuscular Dysplasia (FMD) — non-inflammatory, non-atherosclerotic arteriopathy
→ Location in CNS: Cervical ICA (distal), vertebral artery
→ Mechanism: Alternating medial hyperplasia and thinning → alternating stenosis-dilation
→ CNS Vasculitis: Irregular multifocal stenoses on MRA/DSA (not alternating — more irregular)
→ Differentials:
  Atherosclerosis (proximal, at bifurcations, older patient)
  RCVS (reversible, thunderclap headache, resolves in 3 months)
  CNS vasculitis (irregular, multifocal, CSF pleocytosis)
→ Key clue: Young woman + distal ICA beading + no atherosclerosis risk factors = FMD
→ Complications: Dissection, pseudoaneurysm, stroke
→ Association: Carotid/vertebral dissection in young adults

title: Crescent Sign (SDH vs EDH)
body:
→ SDH: Crescent-shaped hyperdense collection along inner calvarium — concave inner margin following brain surface
→ EDH: Biconvex / lenticular hyperdense collection — lens-shaped, does not cross sutures but can cross midline
→ Why SDH is crescent: Venous origin (bridging veins), no periosteal adherence — spreads freely along convexity
→ Why EDH is biconvex: Arterial origin (middle meningeal artery), periosteum strips away creating lens shape
→ Key differences:
  SDH crosses suture lines (not limited by dural reflections)
  EDH does NOT cross sutures (limited by periosteal attachment)
  SDH can be bilateral; EDH usually unilateral
→ Isodense SDH (subacute): Look for sulcal effacement, medial displacement of cortical veins
→ Indian context: Elderly on aspirin/warfarin — spontaneous SDH common
→ Key clue: Crescent = SDH; Biconvex = EDH

title: Cortical Vein / Gyral Enhancement (Venous Infarct)
body:
→ Appearance: Gyriform / cortical enhancement + T1 cortical hyperintensity in a non-arterial distribution
→ Classic for: Cortical vein thrombosis → venous infarction → cortical luxury perfusion / BBB breakdown
→ Distribution: Does not follow arterial territory — crosses vascular boundaries
→ Associated: Haemorrhagic transformation (petechial/frank), overlying cortical T1 bright signal
→ DWI: Mixed pattern — both vasogenic (ADC elevated) and cytotoxic (ADC reduced) components often coexist; less predictable than arterial infarct
→ MRV: Filling defect in cortical vein or dural sinus
→ Differentials of gyriform enhancement:
  Arterial infarct (follows vascular territory, DWI restriction)
  Encephalitis (herpes — temporal lobe, fever, CSF pleocytosis)
  Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis
  Sturge-Weber (unilateral, pial angioma)
→ Key clue: Haemorrhagic cortical lesion + non-arterial + young woman/postpartum = CVST

title: Ependymal / Subependymal Enhancement Pattern
body:
→ Appearance: Linear or nodular enhancement along ependymal surface of ventricles
→ Classic causes:
  Primary CNS Lymphoma (periventricular infiltration, DWI restriction)
  GBM (subependymal spread — poor prognostic sign)
  CMV encephalitis (immunocompromised)
  Metastatic carcinoma (ependymal/leptomeningeal spread)
→ Differentials:
  Tuberculous meningitis (basal cistern > ependymal, Indian context)
  Ependymoma (intraventricular mass + enhancement)
→ Sequence: Post-contrast T1 (MPRAGE / FLAIR T1 / VIBE) — institution dependent; FLAIR T1 post-contrast may improve leptomeningeal/ependymal conspicuity
→ Key clue: Ependymal enhancement in immunocompetent = lymphoma or GBM; immunocompromised = CMV/lymphoma
→ Pitfall: Normal choroid plexus enhancement should not be mistaken for ependymal disease
→ Indian context: TB meningitis can produce ependymal enhancement — CSF culture mandatory

title: Open Ring Sign (Demyelination)
body:
→ Definition: Incomplete ring enhancement where the open/non-enhancing part faces the grey matter/cortex
→ Classic for: Demyelination — MS, tumefactive demyelination .
→ Direction: Open (non-enhancing) toward GREY MATTER / CORTEX; closed (enhancing) toward WHITE MATTER
→ Mechanism: Advancing demyelination front is in the white matter — this is the enhancing (closed) part; cortex is relatively spared
→ Significance: Helps distinguish from other ring-enhancing lesions — avoids unnecessary biopsy
→ Summary of ring-enhancing lesion patterns:
  Open ring toward cortex (grey matter) = Demyelination
  Thin smooth complete ring + central DWI restriction = Pyogenic abscess
  Thick T2 dark rim + no central DWI = Tuberculoma (target sign)
  Irregular thick ring + necrosis = GBM
  Eccentric target sign (3 zones, post-contrast T1) = Toxoplasmosis
→ Pitfall: Histology of tumefactive demyelination can show bizarre astrocytes + Creutzfeldt cells — may mimic GBM or toxo; imaging open ring sign prevents erroneous biopsy interpretation
→ Key clue: Open part toward cortex = demyelination; this is the opposite of what feels intuitive

title: Primary CNS Lymphoma (PCNSL) — MRI Features
body:
→ Classic appearance: Homogeneous dense enhancement — periventricular, corpus callosum, basal ganglia
→ Key MRI features:
  Dense homogeneous enhancement (immunocompetent) — ring enhancement in immunocompromised
  Periventricular / corpus callosum / basal ganglia location
  DWI restriction throughout lesion (high cellularity)
  T2 iso/hypointense relative to cortex (dense cellularity)
  Often contacts ventricular surface
→ Immunocompromised PCNSL: Ring-enhancing — may mimic toxoplasmosis; distinguish by larger size, solitary, DWI throughout
→ Steroid effect: Dramatic regression — 'ghost tumour' — avoid steroids before biopsy
→ CSF: Cytology low sensitivity (~25%), flow cytometry better
→ Differentials: GBM (necrotic ring, crosses CC), toxoplasmosis (multiple, basal ganglia, HIV), tumefactive MS (open ring)
→ Indian context: TB always in differential — biopsy required if diagnostic uncertainty
→ Key clue: Dense periventricular enhancing mass + DWI restriction throughout + T2 iso/dark = PCNSL

title: Aqueductal Stenosis — MRI Features
body:
→ Note: 'Trident sign' is NOT associated with aqueductal stenosis — it belongs to spinal cord neurosarcoidosis (see separate note)
→ Aqueductal stenosis: Most common cause of non-communicating (obstructive) hydrocephalus in adults
→ MRI findings:
  Dilated lateral ventricles + dilated 3rd ventricle
  Normal-sized 4th ventricle (key distinguishing feature)
  Absent aqueductal flow void on T2 (normal = linear dark flow void on sagittal)
  Transependymal CSF seepage — periventricular T2/FLAIR halo
→ PC-MRI (phase-contrast MRI): Absent/reduced CSF flow through aqueduct — functional gold standard
→ Causes: Congenital web/membrane, tectal glioma (focal T2 bright tectal thickening), pineal mass, post-haemorrhagic/post-meningitic
→ Key anatomical search: Always examine tectal plate for subtle glioma — can be easily missed
→ Treatment: ETV (endoscopic third ventriculostomy) — post-ETV MRI confirms CSF flow through fenestration
→ Key clue: Lat + 3rd dilated, 4th normal = aqueductal level obstruction; find the cause at tectal plate

title: H-shaped Cord Lesion (Anterior Spinal Artery Infarct)
body:
→ Appearance: Axial MRI cord — bilateral anterior horn signal change giving an H or owl-eye pattern
→ Classic for: Anterior spinal artery (ASA) infarct
→ Mechanism: ASA supplies anterior 2/3 of cord — bilateral anterior horns selectively infarcted
→ Sequences: DWI restriction (if high field) + T2 hyperintensity in anterior cord
→ Sagittal: Pencil-like T2 hyperintensity in anterior cord, often multiple levels
→ Differentials of central cord T2 signal:
  Transverse myelitis (central, >2/3 cord cross-section, >3 vertebral segments in NMOSD)
  SACD (posterior columns — dorsal, not anterior)
  Syringomyelia (CSF signal, not T2 signal)
→ Cause: Aortic dissection/surgery, systemic hypotension, vasculitis, disc herniation compressing artery
→ Key clue: Sudden-onset flaccid paraplegia + preserved posterior column (vibration/proprioception intact) = ASA infarct
→ Indian context: Aortic surgery, aortic TB, post-partum

title: Snake Eye Sign (Cervical Myelopathy)
body:
→ Appearance: Axial T2 MRI cord — bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in anterior horns resembling eyes of a snake
→ Classic for: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM)
→ Mechanism: Chronic compressive ischemia → anterior horn cell degeneration
→ Location: Typically C3-C6, at level of maximum compression
→ Significance: Indicates irreversible anterior horn cell loss — correlates with poor surgical outcome
→ Differentiation from H-sign: Snake eye = more focal/punctate anterior horn signal; H-sign = ASA infarct (acute)
→ Associated: Canal stenosis, disc osteophyte complex, T2 cord signal at compression level (myelomalacia)
→ Grading of cord change: T2 signal only (potentially reversible) vs T1 hypointensity (irreversible myelomalacia)
→ Surgical implication: T1 hypointensity = poor prognosis for recovery
→ Key clue: Chronic neck pain + upper motor neuron signs + bilateral anterior horn T2 = CSM with snake eye

title: Comb Sign / Paintbrush Sign (Transverse Myelitis)
body:
→ Appearance: Post-contrast sagittal cord MRI — radially oriented enhancement from cord surface inward like bristles of a comb/paintbrush
→ Classic for: Active transverse myelitis (inflammatory, demyelinating)
→ Mechanism: Blood-cord barrier breakdown at site of active inflammation — enhancement tracks along perivascular spaces
→ Sequences: Sagittal T1+C best; associated T2 cord hyperintensity
→ NMOSD pattern: Longitudinally extensive (>3 segments), central cord, T2 bright, gadolinium enhancement
→ MS cord: Short segment (<2 vertebral segments), peripheral/dorsal, <50% cord cross-section
→ Differentials of cord enhancement:
  Dural AVF (tram-track perimedullary enhancement + flow voids)
  Cord tumor — ependymoma (central, cap sign), astrocytoma (eccentric)
  Cord sarcoidosis (trident/lollipop enhancement)
→ Key clue: Longitudinally extensive T2 + central cord + comb enhancement = NMOSD until proven otherwise
→ Indian context: Devic's / NMOSD more prevalent in Asia; NMO-IgG (AQP4-Ab) testing mandatory

title: Tram Track Enhancement (Spinal Dural AVF)
body:
→ Appearance: Bilateral linear perimedullary enhancement along cord surface on post-contrast T1 — like tram tracks
→ Classic for: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistula (SDAVF)
→ Mechanism: Arterialized venous drainage → venous hypertension → cord ischemia + edema → cord T2 signal
→ Additional findings:
  Prominent perimedullary flow voids (serpiginous vessels on T2)
  Central cord T2 hyperintensity (lower thoracic / conus level)
  Cord swelling (early) → atrophy (late)
→ Location: Most common T6-T10 thoracic dural fistula
→ Definitive: DSA — identifies fistulous point and feeding radicular artery
→ Differentials of longitudinal cord T2: NMOSD, cord tumor, transverse myelitis
→ Key clue: Progressive myelopathy + perimedullary flow voids + tram track enhancement = SDAVF
→ Pitfall: Flow voids can be subtle on standard sequences — dedicated coronal T2 with thin slices
→ Clinical: Middle-aged man, progressive painless leg weakness, Foix-Alajouanine syndrome

title: Pencil-like T2 Cord Signal (SACD)
body:
→ Appearance: Thin linear T2 hyperintensity in posterior columns of cord — pencil-thin on sagittal, inverted V on axial
→ Classic for: Subacute Combined Degeneration (SACD) — Vitamin B12 deficiency
→ Pathology: Demyelination of posterior (dorsal columns) and lateral (corticospinal) columns
→ Axial pattern: Inverted V (posterior columns only) — early; progresses to involve lateral columns (H pattern)
→ Location: Cervical > thoracic
→ Differentials of posterior column T2:
  Copper deficiency myelopathy (identical imaging — look for dietary/gastric bypass history)
  Tabes dorsalis (syphilis — posterior column, but different clinical)
  Friedreich's ataxia (posterior column + cerebellar)
  HIV vacuolar myelopathy
→ Enhancement: Usually absent (non-inflammatory)
→ Lab correlation: Serum B12, MMA, homocysteine
→ Indian context: Vegetarians, elderly, post-gastrectomy patients — B12 deficiency common
→ Key clue: Posterior column T2 + vegetarian/elderly + sensory ataxia = SACD until B12 checked

title: Cap Sign (Spinal Cord Ependymoma)
body:
→ Appearance: T2 hypointense rim at superior and/or inferior poles of an intramedullary cord tumor
→ Classic for: Spinal cord ependymoma (most common intramedullary tumor in adults)
→ Mechanism: Hemosiderin cap from previous hemorrhage within the tumor — paramagnetic = T2 dark
→ Confirms on SWI/GRE: Blooming at tumor poles
→ Associated: Central cord location, cysts at poles (rostral/caudal cyst), intense homogeneous enhancement
→ Differentials of intramedullary mass:
  Astrocytoma (eccentric, ill-defined, less enhancement, no cap sign, children > adults)
  Hemangioblastoma (dorsal, flow voids, associated with VHL, intense enhancement)
  Metastasis (multiple, history of malignancy)
→ Key clue: Central cord + cap sign + cysts + uniform enhancement = ependymoma
→ Pitfall: Cap sign not always present — its absence does not exclude ependymoma
→ Surgery: Well-defined plane — amenable to gross total resection (unlike astrocytoma)

title: DWI Restriction Patterns — Brain Summary
body:
→ DWI restriction = reduced ADC = restricted water motion
→ Cytotoxic edema (cell swelling, membrane pump failure): Acute infarct, abscess cavity, hypercellular tumor
→ Viscous contents: Epidermoid (brilliant DWI restriction — pathognomonic), abscess pus, mucocele
→ High cellularity: PCNSL (throughout lesion), PNET/medulloblastoma, atypical meningioma
→ Specific patterns:
  Acute infarct: Vascular territory, DWI+/ADC- within minutes
  Abscess: Smooth thin ring + central DWI restriction (pus)
  Epidermoid: Follows CSF spaces, brilliant DWI restriction, no enhancement
  CJD: Cortical ribbon + basal ganglia/thalamus
  Hypoglycemia: Posterior limb internal capsule + cortex (unique pattern)
→ Pseudo-restriction: T2 shine-through (high T2 + mildly reduced ADC) — confirm on ADC map
→ Key clue: Always read DWI alongside ADC map — never interpret DWI alone
→ Pitfall: Subacute infarct DWI pseudonormalizes at 10–14 days — T2/FLAIR still abnormal

title: Wernicke Encephalopathy Pattern
body:
→ Appearance: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity + DWI restriction in:
  Mammillary bodies (most specific)
  Periaqueductal grey matter
  Dorsomedial thalami (not pulvinar — pulvinar involvement = vCJD/JE, not Wernicke)
  Tectal plate
→ Classic for: Wernicke encephalopathy — thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency
→ Enhancement: Mammillary body enhancement on post-contrast T1 (acute) — highly specific
→ Clinical triad: Ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + confusion (all three present in only 10–15%)
→ Setting: Chronic alcoholism, malnutrition, prolonged vomiting, post-bariatric surgery, hyperemesis gravidarum
→ Differentials of bilateral thalamic T2:
  Artery of Percheron infarct (bilateral paramedian thalami + midbrain — DWI)
  Deep venous thrombosis (bilateral thalami — internal cerebral vein/straight sinus thrombosis)
  CJD (DWI cortical ribboning + rapid course)
  vCJD (pulvinar sign — bilateral pulvinar, not dorsomedial)
  Japanese encephalitis (bilateral thalami + substantia nigra, fever, India)
→ Key clue: Mammillary body T2/enhancement + alcoholic = Wernicke — treat immediately with IV thiamine
→ Indian context: Wernicke underdiagnosed in alcoholics — give B1 before glucose (glucose precipitates crisis)

title: Artery of Percheron Infarct
body:
→ Appearance: Bilateral paramedian thalamic DWI restriction ± rostral midbrain involvement
→ Classic for: Artery of Percheron (AOP) occlusion — single unpaired artery supplying bilateral paramedian thalami
→ Axial DWI: Butterfly / V-shaped bilateral thalamic restriction
→ With midbrain: Additional DWI restriction in rostral midbrain interpeduncular region
→ Differentials of bilateral thalamic T2/DWI:
  Deep cerebral venous thrombosis (internal cerebral vein / straight sinus — no DWI, more edema)
  Wernicke (mammillary bodies, periaqueductal, T2 not DWI typically)
  Leigh disease (children, basal ganglia + brainstem, metabolic)
  Top of basilar syndrome (additional occipital, cerebellar involvement)
→ MRA: May be normal (AOP too small) — diagnosis often made on DWI pattern
→ Key clue: Bilateral thalamic DWI restriction — think AOP; add midbrain = confirms
→ Clinical: Sudden onset coma / hypersomnolence + vertical gaze palsy + memory impairment

title: FLAIR Vascular Hyperintensity (FVH)
body:
→ Appearance: Hyperintense signal in sulci on FLAIR — resembling vessels / serpiginous
→ Mechanism: Slow blood flow (collateral or ischemic) → incomplete CSF suppression on FLAIR
→ Classic for: Acute large vessel occlusion — retrograde leptomeningeal collateral flow
→ Also seen in: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), meningitis, gadolinium in CSF (post-contrast)
→ In stroke: FVH ipsilateral to occlusion = good collaterals (favorable prognosis)
→ Differentials of sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity:
  SAH (clinical presentation, blood products on GRE)
  Meningitis / TB meningitis (fever, CSF pleocytosis)
  Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis (known malignancy)
  High FiO2 artifact (supplemental O2 suppresses CSF T1 → apparent FLAIR signal)
→ Key clue: Sulcal FLAIR bright + acute stroke = FVH from collaterals (not bleed — confirm on GRE)
→ Pitfall: O2 therapy common in acute stroke — causes false sulcal FLAIR bright

title: Expansion of Splenium — Mildly Restricted DWI
body:
→ Splenium of corpus callosum is a watershed zone — selectively vulnerable to various insults
→ Reversible Splenial Lesion Syndrome (RESLES): Transient DWI restriction in splenium — complete resolution
→ Causes of splenial DWI restriction:
  Seizures (post-ictal)
  Antiepileptic drug withdrawal/addition (classic — valproate, phenytoin)
  Infections (influenza, rotavirus in children)
  Metabolic (hyponatremia correction)
  Marchiafava-Bignami (alcohol — usually more extensive)
→ Key feature: REVERSIBLE — follow-up MRI shows complete resolution (unlike MS or GBM)
→ Differentials: MS plaque in splenium (non-reversible, ovoid, Dawson's fingers elsewhere), GBM (butterfly), lymphoma
→ Key clue: Splenial DWI restriction + seizure history/drug change = RESLES — reassure, follow up
→ Pitfall: Do not mistake for stroke (splenium not in standard arterial territory)

title: Hemosiderin Ring / Popcorn Sign (Cavernoma)
body:
→ Appearance: Mixed T1/T2 signal core (blood products of different ages) + complete hemosiderin rim (T2 dark, blooms on SWI)
→ Classic for: Cerebral cavernous malformation (cavernoma)
→ Pathology: Clustered abnormal capillary-type vessels with repeated microhemorrhages
→ SWI/GRE: Blooming hemosiderin ring — more lesions visible than on T2 alone
→ No enhancement: Typically no enhancement (differentiates from metastasis)
→ No surrounding edema (unless recent bleed)
→ Angiographically occult: Not seen on DSA
→ Familial form: Multiple cavernomas — CCM1 (KRIT1), CCM2, CCM3 mutations
→ Differentials: Metastatic melanoma (T1 bright, enhances), hemorrhagic metastasis (edema, enhancement), DVA (associated with cavernoma)
→ Key clue: Popcorn + hemosiderin ring + no enhancement + SWI blooming = cavernoma
→ Risk: Small annual bleed risk ~0.5%/year (sporadic) — higher if prior bleed or brainstem location

title: Basal Cistern Obliteration (Herniation Signs on CT)
body:
→ Systematic approach to herniation on CT — critical signs to report:
→ Subfalcine herniation: Cingulate gyrus crosses midline under falx → midline shift + ACA compression
→ Uncal / transtentorial herniation:
  Effacement of suprasellar/perimesencephalic cisterns
  Ipsilateral CN III compression (blown pupil)
  Kernohan's notch — contralateral cerebral peduncle compression against tentorium (false localizing)
  Duret hemorrhage — secondary brainstem hemorrhage (poor prognosis)
→ Tonsillar herniation: Cerebellar tonsils below foramen magnum > 5mm (adults) — effaced foramen magnum cistern
→ Upward transtentorial: Posterior fossa mass → upward cerebellar herniation through tentorium
→ Key measurements to report: Midline shift (mm at septum pellucidum), cistern status, ventricle size
→ Key clue: Loss of basal cisterns = critically raised ICP — emergency report
→ Do not miss: Duret hemorrhage (small pontine bleed) = irreversible brainstem injury

title: Enhancement Patterns of CNS Masses — Framework
body:
→ Enhancement = blood-brain barrier breakdown
→ No enhancement: Low grade glioma, epidermoid, DNET, ganglioglioma, cortical dysplasia
→ Homogeneous solid: Meningioma, PCNSL, pilocytic astrocytoma (young), medulloblastoma
→ Heterogeneous: GBM, metastasis (variable)
→ Ring enhancing: Abscess, GBM, metastasis, tuberculoma, tumefactive MS, radiation necrosis
→ Nodular mural: Hemangioblastoma, pilocytic astrocytoma (cyst + nodule)
→ Leptomeningeal: TB meningitis, carcinomatous, lymphoma, sarcoidosis
→ Ependymal: PCNSL, GBM spread, CMV, metastasis
→ Dural based: Meningioma, lymphoma, myeloma, TB pachymeningitis
→ Perivascular / lace-like: PCNSL
→ Key clue: Enhancement pattern alone is never diagnostic — combine with location, DWI, clinical context
→ Indian context: TB always in differential for any ring or leptomeningeal enhancement

title: Temporal Lobe T2 Hyperintensity — Differential Framework
body:
→ Temporal lobe is anatomically vulnerable and a classic teaching area
→ Herpes Simplex Encephalitis (HSV-1):
  Bilateral asymmetric temporal + insular + cingulate T2/DWI
  Spares basal ganglia (unlike others)
  Haemorrhagic component on GRE
  Urgent IV acyclovir — do not wait for CSF
→ Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis:
  MRI may be normal or subtle T2 hippocampal
  Young woman, psychiatric features, ovarian teratoma
→ Limbic encephalitis (LGI1, CASPR2): Unilateral/bilateral hippocampal T2, FAOSC (faciobrachial dystonic seizures — LGI1)
→ Paraneoplastic: Look for underlying malignancy
→ Low grade glioma: Expansile, no enhancement, infiltrative T2
→ DNET: Cortical/subcortical, bubbly, no edema, young patient with seizures
→ Indian context: Viral encephalitis (Japanese Encephalitis — bilateral thalami + temporal, India specific)
→ Key clue: Febrile + temporal T2 + haemorrhage = HSV — treat empirically, image confirms

title: Japanese Encephalitis MRI Pattern
body:
→ Appearance: Bilateral symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in thalami (especially substantia nigra, globus pallidus) + temporal lobe involvement
→ Classic for: Japanese Encephalitis virus (JEV) — Flavivirus, Culex mosquito
→ MRI targets: Thalami (bilateral), substantia nigra, basal ganglia, brainstem, cerebellum, temporal lobes
→ DWI: Restricted diffusion in acute phase
→ Differentials of bilateral thalamic T2:
  AOP infarct (DWI restricted, vascular territory)
  Wernicke (mammillary bodies, periaqueductal)
  Deep CVT (Galenic/straight sinus thrombosis)
  vCJD (pulvinar sign)
  Wilson disease (Panda sign)
→ Indian context: HIGHLY RELEVANT — endemic in Eastern India, Bihar, UP, Assam; seasonal (monsoon)
→ Key clue: Child/young adult + monsoon season + bilateral thalamic T2 + fever = JE until proven otherwise
→ Serology: JEV IgM in CSF/serum — gold standard
→ Prognosis: Bilateral thalamic involvement with DWI restriction = poor outcome

title: Parasitic CNS Lesions — NCC vs Other
body:
→ Neurocysticercosis (NCC) — Taenia solium (pork tapeworm):
  Most common parasitic CNS infection globally
  Stages: Vesicular (cyst + scolex — dot in hole), colloidal (ring enhancement + edema), nodular calcified, calcified
  Scolex = pathognomonic dot within cyst (T2 bright cyst + eccentric hypointense dot)
  Multiple lesions at grey-white junction and basal cisterns
  Racemose NCC — grape-like cysts in basal cisterns, no scolex
→ Echinococcus (Hydatid) — Echinococcus granulosus:
  Large unilocular cyst, daughter cysts, no edema or enhancement of wall (unless ruptured)
  DO NOT aspirate / biopsy — anaphylaxis risk
→ Toxoplasma: Ring enhancing, basal ganglia, AIDS
→ Amoeba (Naegleria/Acanthamoeba): Hemorrhagic necrosis, fulminant
→ Indian context: NCC very common — pig-rearing states, poor sanitation; commonest cause of adult onset seizures in endemic areas
→ Key clue: Cyst + dot (scolex) = NCC; large cyst + daughter cysts + no edema = hydatid
→ Pitfall: Calcified NCC mimics other calcifications — no enhancement = burned out stage

title: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)
body:
→ Appearance: Bilateral symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in posterior cerebral white matter — parieto-occipital predominance
→ Mechanism: Failure of cerebrovascular autoregulation → vasogenic edema
→ Classic settings: Hypertensive emergency, eclampsia/pre-eclampsia, immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus), chemotherapy, sepsis
→ Imaging:
  T2/FLAIR hyperintensity (vasogenic edema — ADC elevated, DWI dark)
  Contrast enhancement variable
  Haemorrhage in 15% (bad prognosis)
  Atypical locations: Frontal, temporal, brainstem, cerebellum (do not exclude PRES)
→ Differentials: Venous infarct (parasagittal), arterial infarct (DWI restriction), encephalitis (fever, CSF)
→ Key clue: ADC elevated (vasogenic) + parieto-occipital + hypertension/eclampsia = PRES
→ Reversibility: Usually reverses in 2–8 weeks with BP control — imaging follow-up confirms
→ Indian context: Eclampsia is common — PRES is a must-know in obstetric neuroimaging
→ Pitfall: DWI restriction in PRES = cytotoxic edema → irreversible injury, poor prognosis

title: Aqueduct Stenosis vs Communicating Hydrocephalus
body:
→ Obstructive (non-communicating): Block within ventricular system → upstream dilatation only
  Aqueductal stenosis: Lat + 3rd dilated, 4th normal
  4th ventricle outlet obstruction: All four dilated
  Foramen of Monro obstruction: Unilateral lateral ventricle dilatation (colloid cyst, subependymoma)
→ Communicating: All ventricles dilated (including 4th) — block at arachnoid granulations or CSF resorption
  NPH (Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus): Ventricular enlargement disproportionate to atrophy, narrow CSF spaces at vertex (tight high convexity), widened Sylvian fissures — Hakim's triad
  Post-meningitis / Post-SAH
→ Key imaging distinction:
  DESH pattern (NPH): Dilated Sylvian fissures + tight high convexity sulci + ventricular enlargement
  Atrophy: Diffuse sulcal widening including vertex
→ PC-MRI: Increased aqueductal stroke volume in NPH
→ Indian context: TB meningitis = commonest cause of communicating hydrocephalus in India
→ Key clue: All 4 ventricles dilated + tight vertex sulci + normal/wide Sylvian fissures = NPH pattern

title: Vein of Galen Malformation
body:
→ Appearance: Large midline serpiginous flow void / T2 dark structure in quadrigeminal cistern on neonatal MRI
→ Not a true vein of Galen — misnomer: dilation of median prosencephalic vein of Markowski (embryonic precursor)
→ Arteriovenous fistulas drain directly into this median vein → massive enlargement
→ Neonatal presentation: High-output cardiac failure (macrocrania, prominent scalp veins, bruit)
→ Older children: Hydrocephalus (compresses aqueduct), developmental delay, headache
→ MRI: Large midline T2 dark structure, aqueductal compression, hydrocephalus, brain parenchymal changes (ischemia)
→ MRA/DSA: Multiple arterial feeders (ACA, PCA, thalamoperforators)
→ Treatment: Endovascular embolization (transarterial or transvenous)
→ Differentials of midline posterior fossa/pineal area cystic lesion:
  Arachnoid cyst (follows CSF, no flow void, no cardiac failure)
  Pineal cyst (pineal location, thin wall, no vessels)
  Quadrigeminal cistern arachnoid cyst
→ Key clue: Neonate + cardiac failure + macrocrania + midline T2 dark mass = Vein of Galen

title: Perinatal Hypoxic Ischemic Injury Patterns
body:
→ Pattern depends on severity and gestational age:
→ Partial prolonged hypoxia (mild-moderate, term):
  Watershed zone injury — parasagittal cortex, periventricular white matter
  MRI: T2 hyperintensity in posterior periventricular white matter
→ Profound acute hypoxia (severe, term):
  Deep grey matter injury — basal ganglia + thalami (posterior putamen, ventrolateral thalamus)
  Corticospinal tracts (posterior limb internal capsule — PLIC)
  DWI restriction in basal ganglia and PLIC
→ Premature (< 32 weeks):
  Periventricular leukomalacia (PVL): Periventricular white matter T2 hyperintensity, cysts, volume loss
  IVH (intraventricular hemorrhage): Grades I-IV
→ MRI timing: Day 3–5 optimal for DWI; T2/T1 changes lag behind DWI
→ Prognostic MRI sign: PLIC T1 signal — normal T1 bright at term = good prognosis; absent = poor
→ Key clue: Term asphyxia + posterior putamen/thalamic DWI + PLIC involvement = severe HII — poor prognosis

title: Pineal Region Masses — Approach
body:
→ Systematic approach — identify key features:
→ Germinoma (most common pineal region tumor):
  Homogeneous enhancing mass enveloping calcification (calcification engulfed, not displaced)
  Young male (10-25 years), elevated CSF/serum bHCG, AFP normal
  Dual lesion: Pineal + suprasellar (pathognomonic for germinoma)
  DWI restriction (hypercellular)
→ Teratoma:
  Heterogeneous — fat, calcium, cyst, soft tissue
  AFP markedly elevated
  Young male
→ Pineocytoma (benign, WHO grade 1):
  Well-defined, may have cystic component, uniform enhancement, older adults
→ Pineoblastoma (malignant, WHO grade 4):
  Ill-defined, aggressive, drop metastases in spine, children
→ Pineal cyst (normal variant):
  Thin regular wall, CSF signal, no solid enhancement (thin rim enhancement = normal)
→ Tectal glioma:
  Focal T2 bright thickening of tectal plate, causes aqueductal stenosis, non-enhancing (usually)
→ Key clue: Engulfed calcification = germinoma; displaced calcification = pineocytoma/blastoma

title: Trident Sign (Spinal Cord Neurosarcoidosis)
body:
→ Appearance: Axial post-contrast T1 spinal cord MRI — central cord enhancement + bilateral dorsal subpial enhancement forming a trident shape
→ Classic for: Spinal cord neurosarcoidosis
→ Mechanism: Sarcoid granulomas preferentially involve central cord and dorsal subpial regions
→ Associated: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis pattern on T2
→ Also described as: Lollipop sign on sagittal (central canal dot + linear dorsal enhancement)
→ Differentials of longitudinal cord T2 + enhancement:
  NMOSD (AQP4-Ab, central cord, >3 segments, no trident)
  SDAVF (perimedullary flow voids, tram track enhancement)
  Cord tumour (ependymoma — cap sign; astrocytoma — eccentric)
→ Systemic features: Hilar lymphadenopathy, skin lesions, uveitis, elevated ACE (not always)
→ Key clue: Trident on axial post-contrast cord MRI = neurosarcoidosis until proven otherwise
→ Confirmation: Tissue biopsy (non-necrotising granulomas); serum/CSF ACE; PET-CT for systemic disease

title: Posterior Fossa Cyst — DWM Spectrum Approach
body:
→ **The four entities (simplest approach):**
**Dandy-Walker Malformation (DWM):** Complete/partial vermian agenesis + cystic dilatation of 4th ventricle + upward displacement of tentorium + torcula above lambdoid. Falx cerebelli absent. Almost always hydrocephalus.
**Dandy-Walker Variant (DWV):** Partial inferior vermian hypoplasia + variable 4th ventricular communication. Tentorium usually normal position.
**Mega Cisterna Magna:** Normal cerebellum + normal 4th ventricle + enlarged cisterna magna (>10mm). Key: intact cerebellar vermis, 4th ventricle communicates freely.
**Arachnoid Cyst:** Does NOT communicate with 4th ventricle on CISS/FIESTA. Scallops adjacent bone. Follows CSF on all sequences.
→ **The discriminators:**
Vermis integrity → DWM (absent) vs MCM (normal)
Tentorial position → DWM (elevated) vs rest (normal)
4th ventricular communication → MCM yes, arachnoid cyst NO
Falx cerebelli → absent in DWM
→ **India context:** DWM often presents late with progressive macrocephaly/hydrocephalus
→ **Report tip:** Always comment on vermis morphology, tentorial position, and whether 4th ventricle is compressed or expanded

title: Sellar/Parasellar Mass — Zone-Based Approach
body:
→ **Zone-based differential (most practical approach):**
**Intrasellar:** Pituitary adenoma (macro vs micro), Rathke cleft cyst (T1 bright/dark, no enhancement), pituitary carcinoma (rare, metastasis)
**Suprasellar:** Craniopharyngioma (child: cystic+calcified; adult: solid papillary), germinoma (young male, DI, pineal synchronous), optic/hypothalamic glioma (child, NF1 association), meningioma (dural tail, homogeneous), aneurysm (flow void, pulsation artefact)
**Parasellar (cavernous sinus):** Meningioma, nerve sheath tumour (V, III), metastasis, carotid artery lesion, lymphoma, IgG4 disease
**Infrasellar/Clivus:** Chordoma (T2 very bright, bone destruction, midline), chondrosarcoma (off-midline!), metastasis
→ **Key differentiators:**
Age: Child → craniopharyngioma, germinoma, glioma. Adult → pituitary adenoma, meningioma
DI → germinoma, craniopharyngioma (suprasellar)
Calcification → craniopharyngioma (adamantinomatous > papillary)
T1 bright lesion — lipid cyst, Rathke, subacute blood, craniopharyngioma cyst content
→ **Always assess:** Optic chiasm compression, cavernous sinus invasion, carotid artery encasement (>50% = invasion, not just contact)

title: Leptomeningeal vs Pachymeningeal Enhancement — Approach
body:
→ **Pachymeningeal (dura-arachnoid):** Smooth, linear, dural thickening and enhancement. Does NOT extend into sulci. The pia is spared.
*Causes:* Intracranial hypotension (post-LP, CSF leak — look for sagging brainstem, subdural collections, dural thickening), dural mets, meningioma en-plaque, sarcoidosis, IgG4 disease, post-surgical
→ **Leptomeningeal (pia-arachnoid):** Enhancement within sulci, cisterns, cortical surface. FLAIR bright sulci (sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity) is a key indirect sign.
*Causes:* Bacterial/TB meningitis, carcinomatous meningitis, fungal (cryptococcus), SAH (subacute), NCC, viral encephalitis, neurosarcoidosis
→ **India context:** TB meningitis = most common cause of leptomeningeal enhancement → look for basal exudates, hydrocephalus, infarcts (perforator territory)
→ **Critical differentiator:** Intracranial hypotension = pachymeningeal. TB/carcinomatosis = leptomeningeal.
→ **Sulcal FLAIR bright — DDx:** Leptomeningeal disease, SAH, high O2 therapy (artefact), PRES (cortical), moyamoya (ivy sign)
→ **Report tip:** Post-contrast T1 FLAIR (VIBE/MPRAGE) is far more sensitive than standard post-contrast T1 for leptomeningeal disease

title: Ischemic Stroke — CT and MRI Staging
body:
→ **CT evolution:**
Hyperacute (<6h): Normal CT or subtle loss of grey-white differentiation, loss of insular ribbon, hyperdense MCA sign
Acute (6–24h): Hypodensity appears, sulcal effacement
Subacute (1–7d): Hypodensity well-defined, mass effect peaks 3–5 days, may see gyral enhancement (BBB breakdown)
Chronic: Encephalomalacia, ex-vacuo dilatation, CSF density core
→ **MRI evolution:**
Hyperacute: **DWI +ve, ADC −ve** (cytotoxic oedema). FLAIR may be normal in first 6h → DWI-FLAIR mismatch = wake-up stroke indicator
Acute: FLAIR becomes positive (vasogenic + cytotoxic)
Subacute early (days 1–7): T1 dark, T2/FLAIR bright, gyral enhancement
Subacute late (1–3 weeks): T1 bright core (petechial haemorrhage/methemoglobin). **Fogging** on DWI (pseudo-normalisation of DWI)
Chronic: T1/T2 bright (gliosis rim), dark core (encephalomalacia), wallerian degeneration in corticospinal tracts
→ **Key pearls:**
DWI-ADC mismatch = true restriction (acute infarct). DWI bright + ADC bright = T2 shine-through (subacute/chronic)
PWI-DWI mismatch = salvageable penumbra (target for thrombolysis)
Fogging phenomenon: DWI pseudo-normalises 1–3 weeks → FLAIR still bright, identifies true infarct

title: Territorial vs Watershed vs Lacunar Infarct — Differentiation
body:
→ **Territorial (large vessel) infarct:**
Location: Cortex + subcortical white matter, follows arterial territory
Mechanism: Embolic or thrombotic occlusion of major vessel
MRI: Wedge-shaped, involves cortex, DWI bright in distribution of MCA/ACA/PCA
Clue: Hyperdense MCA sign, cortical ribbon infarct
→ **Watershed (border-zone) infarct:**
Location: Boundary between ACA-MCA (anterior watershed) or MCA-PCA (posterior watershed). Also deep watershed between deep perforators and superficial cortical branches.
*String-of-beads* appearance along anterior watershed = striatocapsular infarcts (carotid stenosis/hypotension)
Mechanism: Haemodynamic (hypoperfusion, carotid stenosis). Think systemic hypotension, cardiac arrest, carotid stenosis.
MRI: Linear, elongated infarcts along watershed zones
→ **Lacunar infarct:**
Location: Basal ganglia, internal capsule, thalamus, pons, deep white matter (NOT cortex)
Size: <15mm (by definition)
Mechanism: Small vessel disease (lipohyalinosis), hypertension-related
MRI: Small DWI bright lesion, old ones = T2/FLAIR bright foci (white matter hyperintensities)
→ **India context:** Lacunar infarcts common in hypertensive population. Watershed infarcts in bilateral symmetrical distribution → think cardiac arrest / severe hypotension

title: Cerebral Venous Thrombosis — Imaging Evolution
body:
→ **CT — direct signs:**
Acute (Day 1–5): **Dense sinus sign** (hyperdense on NCCT). Cord sign = hyperdense cortical vein.
Subacute (Day 5–15): Sinus becomes isodense → most commonly missed phase
Post-contrast: **Empty delta sign** (filling defect in superior sagittal sinus on axial post-contrast CT)
→ **MRI — sinus signal evolution:**
Acute: T1 iso, T2 dark (deoxyhaemoglobin)
Subacute: **T1 bright, T2 bright** (methemoglobin) — most conspicuous
Chronic: Variable; recanalized sinus may appear normal
→ **MRV:** Loss of flow signal in affected sinus. Best sequence for sinus assessment. Compare with NCCT density.
→ **Indirect signs (consequence):**
Venous infarct: Does NOT follow arterial territory, involves white matter + cortex, often haemorrhagic (50%), bilateral in deep CVT (thalamic infarcts = bilateral = think straight sinus or vein of Galen thrombosis)
Diffuse brain swelling without mass lesion
→ **India context:** Young women on OCP, post-partum, dehydration, hypercoagulable states (SLE, factor V Leiden) → high index of suspicion
→ **Key pearl:** Bilateral thalamic infarcts = straight sinus or deep venous system thrombosis until proven otherwise

title: Periventricular vs Subcortical White Matter Disease — Approach
body:
→ **Periventricular predominance:**
MS (Dawson's fingers — perpendicular to ventricles)
SVD/Small vessel ischemic disease (confluent, irregular, elderly+hypertensive)
HIV encephalopathy (diffuse periventricular T2 signal)
CMV (periventricular enhancement + calcification in congenital)
→ **Subcortical U-fibre involvement (subcortical, sparing periventricular):**
ADEM (monophasic, post-infectious, enhancing at onset, multifocal)
PML (JC virus, CD4<200, no enhancement, subcortical + U-fibre, striking asymmetry)
Canavan disease (macrocephaly, NAA↑ on MRS, subcortical U-fibres early)
MRX (subcortical calcification + WM abnormality)
→ **Both periventricular + subcortical:**
MS (classic)
Sarcoidosis (periventricular + leptomeningeal)
CNS lymphoma (periventricular, DWI restricts)
→ **Pattern recognition tips:**
*Tumefactive lesion + WM disease → tumefactive MS (open ring, no mass effect)*
*Butterfly/central pattern → GBM or lymphoma*
*Subcortical + U-fibre spared → SVD/ischaemia*
*Subcortical + U-fibre involved → ADEM, PML, leukodystrophy*
→ **Always check:** Enhancement pattern, DWI restriction, clinical context (age, immunocompetence)

title: Toxic/Metabolic Leukoencephalopathy — Approach
body:
→ **Symmetric WM involvement = metabolic/toxic until proven otherwise**
→ **Pattern by location:**
**Central pons ± extrapontine (ODS):** Rapid correction of hyponatremia → osmotic demyelination syndrome. Central pontine (trident/bat wing on axial MRI). Extrapontine: basal ganglia, thalami, cerebellum. DWI restricts acutely. May lag on T2 by 1–2 weeks. (Also see Trident Sign in existing entries.)
**Bilateral globus pallidus T2 bright:**
CO poisoning (T2 bright GP bilaterally, also WM)
Cyanide, methanol, hypoxia
Wilson disease (GP + putamen, T1 dark)
**Periventricular/diffuse WM:**
Methotrexate leukoencephalopathy (periventricular, DWI restriction possible)
Cyclosporine/PRES (parieto-occipital FLAIR, see PRES entry)
HIV/HAART
**Corpus callosum splenium:**
Anti-epileptic drug withdrawal, infections, hypoglycaemia (see RESLES — existing entry #60)
→ **Key principle:** History is EVERYTHING — chemo, toxic exposure, Na correction rate, CO/cyanide exposure
→ **India context:** CO poisoning in winter (heaters), methanol ingestion (spurious alcohol) → bilateral GP involvement is characteristic

title: CNS Tuberculosis — Full Imaging Spectrum
body:
→ **Full spectrum — know all presentations:**
**TB Meningitis:** Basal exudates (T1 bright/FLAIR bright in basal cisterns, enhancement), communicating hydrocephalus, cranial nerve involvement (II, III, IV, VI, VII). Vasculitis → perforator territory infarcts (lenticulostriate, thalamoperforator)
**Tuberculoma:** Target sign (T2 dark ring, central T2 bright nidus/calcification, ring enhancement). Supratentorial > infratentorial. Solitary or multiple. DWI: does NOT restrict (unlike pyogenic abscess → key differentiator!)
**TB Abscess:** Rare. Liquefaction of tuberculoma. **DWI restricts** (unlike tuberculoma). Ring enhancement, thick irregular wall.
**Tuberculous encephalitis (TBHE):** Diffuse WM T2 hyperintensity, cortical involvement. **Paradoxical reaction** — worsens despite treatment (immune reconstitution)
**Ventriculitis:** Ependymal enhancement, debris in ventricles
→ **India context:** Always first differential in any ring-enhancing lesion, basal meningitis, communicating hydrocephalus, or perforator infarct in young patient
→ **Key MRS tip:** Tuberculoma shows lipid peak (mycolyl) at 0.9ppm — differentiates from metastasis (which shows choline peak)
→ **Report essentials:** Basal exudates, hydrocephalus, infarcts, enhancement pattern, DWI status

title: Neurocysticercosis — Stage-Based MRI
body:
→ **Stage 1 — Vesicular (viable cyst):**
CSF-signal cyst (T1 dark, T2 bright, FLAIR dark), **scolex visible** (eccentric T2 dark nodule = pathognomonic), NO enhancement, NO perilesional oedema. Active living parasite — no immune response.
→ **Stage 2 — Colloidal vesicular (degenerating):**
Cyst contents become T1 bright (proteinaceous), **ring enhancement**, marked perilesional oedema. Scolex may or may not be visible. This is when the patient becomes symptomatic (seizures, headache)
→ **Stage 3 — Granular nodular:**
Shrinking cyst, nodular/ring enhancement, partial calcification. Oedema decreasing.
→ **Stage 4 — Nodular calcified (inactive):**
**SWI/GRE blooming** (haemosiderin/calcium), NO enhancement, NO oedema, NO scolex. Dead parasite. Seizure risk persists due to gliosis.
→ **Only stages 2 and 3 are active → respond to antiparasitic treatment (Albendazole)**
→ **India context:** Endemic. Commonest cause of new-onset seizures in young adults. Pork tapeworm (Taenia solium) lifecycle.
→ **Special forms:**
Racemose NCC: Subarachnoid space, grapes-like appearance, NO scolex, aggressive, basal meningitis
Spinal NCC: Rare, intradural extramedullary
→ **Key differentiator:** NCC stage 1 vs arachnoid cyst — scolex + no mass effect + parenchymal location favours NCC

title: Herpes Encephalitis vs Japanese Encephalitis — Differentiation
body:
→ **HSV Encephalitis (Herpes simplex type 1):**
Location: **Medial temporal lobe, insular cortex, cingulate gyrus** — limbic system predilection
Laterality: Typically unilateral or markedly asymmetric bilateral
MRI: T2/FLAIR bright, DWI restriction (cytotoxic pattern), haemorrhage (T2* dark foci) in 50%+
Enhancement: Variable (leptomeningeal or cortical, late finding)
Clinical: Immunocompetent adult, acute febrile encephalopathy, behavioural changes, seizures, rapid deterioration
Pearl: Spares basal ganglia (unlike JE). Involvement of insula + temporal = HSV until proven otherwise.
→ **Japanese Encephalitis (Flavivirus):**
Location: **Bilateral thalami** (most characteristic), basal ganglia, substantia nigra, cerebral cortex, brainstem, rarely cerebellum
Laterality: Bilateral and symmetric
MRI: T2/FLAIR bright thalami, DWI ± restriction, T2* haemorrhage possible
Clinical: **India-endemic (monsoon season, Culex mosquito), rural area, children + young adults**, acute febrile encephalopathy, movement disorder (extrapyramidal features), high mortality
→ **Key differentiator table:**
Insula/temporal involvement → HSV
Bilateral thalami + basal ganglia → JE (India: monsoon context)
Haemorrhage → both possible but more HSV
Fever + rural India + monsoon → think JE first
→ **Other bilateral thalamic lesions:** CVT (straight sinus), artery of Percheron infarct, Wernicke, basilar top syndrome

title: HIV CNS Complications — CD4-Based Approach
body:
→ **CD4 count-based framework:**
**CD4 >500:** HIV encephalopathy (diffuse symmetric periventricular WM T2 signal, cerebral atrophy)
**CD4 200–500:**
Primary CNS Lymphoma (PCNSL): Periventricular, solitary or multifocal, DWI restricts, homogeneous enhancement. EBV-related.
Toxoplasma encephalitis: Ring-enhancing lesions, basal ganglia/junction of grey-white matter, multiple, T1 dark centre, DWI usually does NOT restrict (abscess-like)
**CD4 <200:**
PML (JC virus): Subcortical asymmetric WM, U-fibres involved, NO enhancement (unless IRIS), DWI restriction at leading edge, no mass effect. Parieto-occipital + frontal predilection.
Cryptococcal meningitis: Basal meningitis, gelatinous pseudocysts in perivascular spaces (enlarged VR spaces especially basal ganglia), minimal enhancement
**CD4 <50:**
CMV encephalitis/ventriculitis: Periventricular ependymal enhancement, subependymal signal
→ **Key differentiators in HIV ring-enhancing lesion:**
Toxoplasma: Multiple, basal ganglia, responds to empirical treatment in 2 weeks
PCNSL: Usually solitary, periventricular, DWI restricts, EBV DNA in CSF
→ **IRIS (Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome):** Paradoxical worsening after HAART. PML-IRIS shows new enhancement at PML lesion. Crypto-IRIS causes worsening meningitis.
→ **India context:** HIV prevalence, co-infection with TB critical — TB + HIV = most common CNS dual infection

title: WHO 2021 CNS Tumor Classification — Key Imaging Implications
body:
→ **The fundamental shift:** Molecular markers now define tumor type and grade, not just histomorphology alone. Radiologists must know these to accurately communicate with clinicians.
→ **Key molecular markers to know:**
**IDH mutation:** IDH-mutant gliomas have better prognosis. On MRI: frontal lobe preference, T2/FLAIR bright, less necrosis/heterogeneity than IDH-wildtype (GBM)
IDH-wildtype astrocytoma = WHO grade 4 (GBM equivalent) even if histology looks low-grade
**1p/19q codeletion:** Defines oligodendroglioma. Frontal lobe, calcification, T2 heterogeneous soap bubble pattern, less DWI restriction
**H3K27M mutation:** Diffuse midline glioma, WHO grade 4. Thalamus, pons (DIPG), spinal cord. **Expansile T2 bright lesion in midline = H3K27M until proven otherwise in children/young adults**
**TERT promoter + EGFR amplification + +7/−10:** GBM markers
→ **Practical imaging implications:**
Frontal lobe T2 lesion, young adult, no necrosis → IDH-mutant astrocytoma (better prognosis, different treatment)
Butterfly GBM through corpus callosum → IDH-wildtype
Pontine expansile lesion in child → DIPG/H3K27M (DO NOT biopsy routinely, MRI/clinical diagnosis)
Calcified frontal glioma + young patient → oligodendroglioma (1p/19q)
→ **Pearl:** Radiomics and MR spectroscopy can suggest IDH status — IDH-mutant gliomas show 2-HG peak on MRS (not routinely available)

title: Meningioma — Benign vs Aggressive MRI Features
body:
→ **Benign (WHO grade 1) features:**
CSF cleft between tumour and brain (extra-axial)
Hyperostosis of adjacent bone
Dural tail (non-specific but supportive)
Homogeneous enhancement
T2 hypointense (fibrous/meningothelial types)
Calcification (psammoma bodies on CT)
Well-defined, hemispheric base
→ **Aggressive (WHO grade 2–3) / Brain invasion features:**
Loss of CSF cleft
Irregular margins
Heterogeneous signal (necrosis, haemorrhage)
Brain oedema out of proportion to tumour size
Restriction on DWI (higher cellularity)
Rapid growth on serial imaging
Bony destruction (NOT hyperostosis)
Sinus invasion with complete occlusion
Multiple lesions (grade 3 can seed)
→ **Special types to know:**
Angiomatous meningioma: Very T2 bright (can mimic glioma), multiple flow voids
Microcystic meningioma: Very T2 bright, young women
Secretory meningioma: Intratumoral pseudopsammoma bodies, marked oedema
→ **India context:** Meningiomas often present late due to slow growth. Always check sinus patency (MRV) before surgery — superior sagittal sinus invasion changes surgical approach.
→ **Report tip:** Comment on dural sinus involvement, cortical vein occlusion, and proximity to eloquent cortex

title: Pediatric Posterior Fossa Tumors — Age-Based Approach
body:
→ **The four main entities and their key differentiators:**
**Pilocytic Astrocytoma (JPA):** Most common cerebellar tumour in children (5–15 years). Cyst + enhancing mural nodule (cyst wall does NOT enhance → needs resection only of nodule). T2 very bright, DWI usually does NOT restrict. Minimal oedema. Excellent prognosis.
**Medulloblastoma:** Most common malignant posterior fossa tumour (peak 3–8 years). Midline vermis, fills/fills-invades 4th ventricle. **DWI restricts** (high cellularity). Homogeneous enhancement, calcification possible. Metastasises via CSF → always do spine MRI + CSF cytology.
**Ependymoma:** Plastic tumour, extends through foramina of Luschka (cerebellopontine angle) and Magendie. Calcification in 50% on CT. DWI variable. Age: typically <5 years (infratentorial). T2 heterogeneous, mixed signal enhancement.
**AT/RT (Atypical Teratoid/Rhabdoid):** <3 years (very young infant). Aggressive, heterogeneous, looks like medulloblastoma but in younger child, larger, INI1 gene mutation. DWI restricts. Haemorrhage common.
→ **Age guide:**
<3 years: AT/RT, ependymoma
3–10 years: JPA, medulloblastoma
>10 years: JPA, haemangioblastoma (VHL association, cyst + nodule but in adult, prominent flow voids)
→ **Key differentiator:** DWI restriction + midline + young child → medulloblastoma until proven otherwise
→ **Always:** Full spine MRI to exclude drop metastases (medulloblastoma, AT/RT, ependymoma)

title: Vertebral Body Collapse — Benign vs Malignant Differentiation
body:
→ **Benign (osteoporotic) collapse:**
Convex posterior cortex preserved
No soft tissue epidural/paraspinal component
Bone-in-bone pattern on CT (band of sclerosis)
T1 signal: **Fat preservation** — T1 bright marrow on fat-suppressed sequences → isointense to normal marrow (fat still present within fracture)
DWI: Low signal (ADC high = benign)
No posterior element involvement
Contiguous levels may be affected
Single level more common in osteoporosis
→ **Malignant collapse:**
Bulging/destruction of posterior cortex
**Epidural or paraspinal soft tissue component** (most discriminating)
Posterior element involvement (pedicle, spinous process)
T1: Complete replacement of marrow fat → T1 dark throughout
DWI: **Restricts** (ADC low = malignant, high cellularity)
Multiple non-contiguous levels (skip metastases)
Known primary malignancy
→ **MRI sequences of choice:** T1 (fat vs tumour), STIR (oedema), DWI+ADC, post-contrast fat-sat T1
→ **India context:** TB spondylitis (Pott's disease) — disc involvement, paraspinal cold abscess, may mimic malignancy but involves disc space (malignancy spares disc). Subligamentous spread under ALL.
→ **Pearl:** Involvement of posterior elements (pedicles) = malignancy until proven otherwise. Benign fractures spare posterior elements.

title: Skull Base Lesion — Location-Based Approach
body:
