→ **Anterior skull base (olfactory groove / cribriform plate / planum sphenoidale):**
Meningioma (olfactory groove, planum)
Esthesioneuroblastoma/Olfactory neuroblastoma: Straddles cribriform plate, dumbbell shape, intracranial + nasal component, Kadish staging
Sinonasal carcinoma with intracranial extension
→ **Middle skull base (sella / sphenoid / cavernous sinus):**
Pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma (see sellar approach pearl)
Chordoma: Midline, T2 very bright (chordoid matrix), aggressive bone destruction, crosses disc spaces
Chondrosarcoma: OFF-MIDLINE (petroclival junction), T2 bright, calcification, locally aggressive
Fibrous dysplasia: Ground glass bone expansion
Metastasis
→ **Posterior skull base (clivus / CPA / jugular foramen):**
CPA: Schwannoma (internal auditory canal extension, T2 bright, ice cream cone), Meningioma (broader base, calcification, dural tail), Epidermoid (DWI restricts, scallops adjacent bone)
Jugular foramen: Glomus jugulare (salt and pepper on MRI, flow voids, aggressive bone destruction), Schwannoma
Clivus: Chordoma (see above), metastasis, plasmacytoma
→ **Key differentiators:**
Midline clivus destruction → chordoma vs mets vs plasmacytoma
Off-midline petroclival → chondrosarcoma (T2 bright + calcification)
CPA mass → measure IAC extension (schwannoma) vs dural attachment (meningioma) vs DWI restriction (epidermoid)
→ **Always comment:** Bone destruction pattern, cranial nerve foramina involvement, vascular encasement

title: Corpus Callosum Mass — Approach
body:
→ **The corpus callosum is a crossroads — multiple pathologies pass through it**
→ **Segment-based pathology clues:**
Genu: MS plaques, trauma (DAI), lymphoma
Body: GBM butterfly (through splenium), lymphoma (midline)
Splenium: Metabolic (ODS reversal), infection, CLN, DNET (rare), MS
Rostrum: NPC type C
→ **Main differentials:**
**GBM (Butterfly glioma):** Infiltrates corpus callosum, bilateral hemispheres. T2 heterogeneous, central necrosis, irregular ring enhancement. Mass effect. DWI restriction at solid portion.
**Primary CNS Lymphoma:** Periventricular/callosal, DWI restricts (high cellularity), **homogeneous enhancement** (unlike GBM ring), T2 hypointense relative to oedema. Responds to steroids (transient — don't give before biopsy!)
**Tumefactive MS:** Incomplete/open ring enhancement (open side faces grey matter). T2 bright WM, other MS lesions elsewhere, no mass effect. Venous sinuses pattern of enhancement.
**Lipoma:** T1 bright (fat signal), fat suppression dropout. Chemical shift artefact. Midline, pericallosal location common. Associated callosal dysgenesis.
**Infarct:** Pericallosal artery territory. DWI restricts acutely. No mass effect after acute phase.
→ **Pearl:** Before calling a callosal mass lymphoma, look for other enhancing lesions and check steroid history (steroids cause lymphoma to vanish transiently = 'ghost tumour' → always biopsy before starting steroids)

title: Ring-Enhancing Lesion — Approach (India Context)
body:
→ **Mnemonic: MAGIC DR** — Metastasis, Abscess, Glioblastoma, Infarct (subacute), Contusion, Demyelination, Radiation necrosis
→ **India-specific priority order:**
TB abscess / Tuberculoma → NCC (colloidal stage) → Pyogenic abscess → Metastasis → GBM
→ **Key differentiators:**
**DWI restriction (abscess centre):**
Restricts → Pyogenic abscess (viscous pus, high cellularity). Also TB abscess.
Does NOT restrict → Tuberculoma, metastasis, GBM necrotic centre (ADC high)
**Wall characteristics:**
Thin, smooth, uniform → abscess (pyogenic)
Thick, irregular, nodular → GBM, metastasis
Target sign (T2 dark ring + central bright) → tuberculoma
Open ring (incomplete, opens toward grey matter) → Demyelination (MS)
**Number and location:**
Solitary, deep grey-white junction → metastasis
Multiple, cortical/subcortical → metastasis, NCC
Basal ganglia/thalamus → toxoplasma, TB, metastasis
**MRS:**
Lipid at 0.9ppm → tuberculoma (mycolyl lipid), GBM necrosis
Choline↑, NAA↓ → glioma (solid portion)
Amino acids (alanine/valine at 0.9ppm) → pyogenic abscess
→ **India context:** Never dismiss solitary ring-enhancing lesion as metastasis without TB/NCC workup. Anti-TB trial + repeat MRI at 6–8 weeks is acceptable practice for presumed tuberculoma in endemic settings when biopsy is high-risk.

title: Spinal Cord Lesion — Intramedullary vs Extramedullary Approach
body:
→ **Step 1: Localise — intramedullary (IM) vs intradural extramedullary (IDEM) vs extradural (ED)**
→ **Intramedullary (within cord):**
Cord expansion, epicentre within cord, cord surrounded by CSF at level of lesion
Main DDx: Ependymoma, astrocytoma, metastasis, demyelination, AVM/cavernoma
**Ependymoma:** Most common IM tumour in adults. Cervical > thoracic. **Cap sign** (haemosiderin caps at poles — T2* dark). Central location. Homogeneous enhancement. Cysts at poles.
**Astrocytoma:** Children > adults. Eccentric, ill-defined, no cap sign. Heterogeneous enhancement.
**Hemangioblastoma:** Cyst + mural nodule (resembles JPA in brain). VHL association. Flow voids. Thoracolumbar.
**Demyelination (MS):** Short segment (<2 vertebral levels), peripheral location (posterior/lateral), <50% cord cross-section. Acute = enhances.
**NMOSD:** Long segment (≥3 vertebral levels), central grey matter, area postrema in brainstem. AQP4-IgG.
→ **Intradural Extramedullary (IDEM):**
Cord displaced AWAY from lesion, CSF cap on one side
**Schwannoma:** Extends through neural foramen (dumbbell), T2 bright, intense enhancement, may show cystic change
**Meningioma:** Broad dural base, T2 isointense/dark, homogeneous enhancement, calcification, thoracic > cervical, middle-aged women
→ **Extradural:**
Cord displaced, epidural fat effaced
Metastasis (vertebral body primary), abscess (TB cold abscess, posterior paraspinal extension), disc herniation
→ **India context:** TB epidural abscess + vertebral collapse = most common cause of cord compression in India. Always look for adjacent vertebral body changes.

title: Mesial Temporal Sclerosis — MRI Diagnosis
body:
→ **MTS is the commonest cause of refractory temporal lobe epilepsy in adults**
→ **MRI protocol essentials:** Coronal T2, coronal FLAIR, and coronal T1 IR (inversion recovery) through hippocampus perpendicular to long axis. 3D volumetric sequences for quantification.
→ **Four cardinal MRI findings (all four = classic MTS):**
1. **T2/FLAIR hyperintensity** of hippocampus (internal architecture loss)
2. **Hippocampal atrophy** — compare right vs left on coronal slices
3. **Loss of internal digitations** of hippocampus on T2 (normal corrugated texture lost)
4. **Loss of T1 hypointensity** (grey matter T1 signal uniformly dark — sclerosed)
→ **Associated findings:** Atrophy of ipsilateral fornix, mammillary body, entorhinal cortex, amygdala, temporal pole WM signal change
→ **Bilateral MTS:** More common than previously thought — look carefully at both sides
→ **FDG-PET:** Hypometabolism in ipsilateral temporal lobe — larger zone of hypometabolism than MRI lesion (functional deficit zone)
→ **Key pitfall:** MRI may be normal in up to 30% of proven MTS — do not exclude if clinical suspicion high. 3T MRI preferred.
→ **Differential of T2 bright hippocampus:** MTS, limbic encephalitis (bilateral, anti-LGI1, anti-NMDA), HSV encephalitis (unilateral + haemorrhage), glioma, DNET
→ **Report tip:** Always comment on size asymmetry, internal architecture, and signal. Compare sides systematically.

title: Raised Intracranial Pressure and Herniation — Imaging Approach
body:
→ **CT signs of raised ICP:**
Sulcal effacement, loss of grey-white differentiation
Basilar cistern obliteration (early: compressed, late: absent)
Midline shift measurement — at level of septum pellucidum to midline (>5mm = significant)
Duret haemorrhages — pontine/midbrain linear haemorrhages from downward herniation (stretching perforators) = very poor prognosis
→ **Herniation patterns:**
**Subfalcine (cingulate):** Cingulate gyrus shifts under falx. ACA compression → medial frontal/parietal infarct.
**Uncal (transtentorial downward):** Medial temporal lobe (uncus) over tentorium. CN III compression (ipsilateral dilated pupil). PCA compression → occipital infarct. Duret haemorrhage = advanced.
**Upward (upward transtentorial):** Posterior fossa mass pushes cerebellum/vermis upward through tentorium. Compresses superior vermis, midbrain. Seen with posterior fossa tumours/haemorrhage.
**Tonsillar:** Cerebellar tonsils herniate through foramen magnum. Compresses medulla. Fatal if severe. Tonsils >5mm below foramen magnum = Chiari I (chronic, often asymptomatic); acute herniation = emergency.
**Transalar (sphenoid):** Frontal lobe displaced posteriorly over sphenoid ridge — rare.
→ **Pseudotumour cerebri (IIH):** Empty sella, optic nerve sheath enlargement (>5.5mm on axial MRI), posterior globe flattening, tortuous optic nerves, transverse sinus stenosis, small ventricles. Young obese women.
→ **Report tip:** Always report cistern patency + herniation type + Duret haemorrhages + midline shift together as integrated assessment.

title: Extra-Axial Haemorrhage — EDH vs SDH vs SAH
body:
→ **Extradural Haematoma (EDH):**
Shape: **Biconvex (lenticular)** — does not cross suture lines (dura attached to sutures)
Location: Temporal (MC) — middle meningeal artery. Can also be venous (posterior fossa, sagittal sinus)
Density: Hyperdense acute. Swirl sign = active bleeding (mixed density).
Cross sutures: NO (unless suture diastasis in children)
Classic history: Lucid interval + deterioration (arterial bleed)
→ **Subdural Haematoma (SDH):**
Shape: **Crescent** — follows brain contour, crosses suture lines freely
Cross sutures: YES
Cross midline: NO (stopped by falx)
Density evolution: Acute = hyperdense. Subacute (1–3 wk) = isodense (can be missed — look for sulcal effacement, midline shift, displaced grey-white junction). Chronic = hypodense.
Hyperdense within isodense SDH = acute-on-chronic (re-bleed)
MRI: FLAIR most sensitive for isodense SDH
→ **Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH):**
Location: Fills sulci, basal cisterns, sylvian fissures
Density: Hyperdense in sulci on NCCT (blood in CSF spaces)
MRI: FLAIR sulcal hyperintensity (most sensitive after 24h). Acute SAH: GRE/SWI sensitive.
Distribution clue: Basal/diffuse = aneurysmal. Perimesencephalic = non-aneurysmal (good prognosis).
CT angiogram: Mandatory to exclude aneurysm
→ **India context:** Head trauma (road traffic accidents = commonest), anticoagulant use in elderly → SDH. SAH = worst headache of life — always CTA.
→ **Key differentiator:** EDH lenticular, does not cross suture lines. SDH crescent, crosses sutures but not midline.

title: Cerebral Vasculitis — Imaging Approach
body:
→ **Imaging findings (non-specific but suggestive):**
Multiple bilateral infarcts in different vascular territories (cortical + subcortical)
Multiple ages of infarcts (DWI + FLAIR + T2 + T1 — different stages simultaneously)
Young patient with stroke, no cardiac source, no large vessel atherosclerosis
Enhancement of vessel walls on high-resolution vessel wall MRI (VW-MRI)
→ **Vessel wall MRI (3T, black blood sequences):**
Concentre vessel wall enhancement = vasculitis, atherosclerosis (eccentric)
Smooth concentric enhancement of arteries of Willis → primary CNS vasculitis, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
Eccentric wall thickening/enhancement → atherosclerosis
→ **Angiographic pattern (CTA/MRA/DSA):**
Beaded appearance (alternating stenosis and dilatation) = string of beads sign = vasculitis or FMD
FMD: Medium vessels (renal, carotid), young woman, non-inflammatory
Vasculitis: Multiple segmental stenoses, entire arterial tree
→ **Primary CNS vasculitis (PCNSV):** Small vessel, often angio-negative. Brain biopsy needed.
→ **Secondary causes (India context):**
TB vasculitis (perforator territory infarcts, basal meningitis)
Fungal (Aspergillus, mucor — immunocompromised)
Herpes zoster vasculopathy
SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome
→ **RCVS:** Thunderclap headache, post-partum, serotonergic drugs. Diffuse vasospasm resolves in weeks. Convexity SAH. No vessel wall enhancement (unlike vasculitis).
→ **Key pearl:** RCVS mimics vasculitis on angiography but vessel wall MRI shows NO enhancement in RCVS. Clinical history critical.

title: Pituitary Apoplexy and Incidentaloma — Approach
body:
→ **Pituitary Apoplexy:**
Acute haemorrhage or infarction into pituitary adenoma
Clinical: Sudden severe headache, visual disturbance (CN III, chiasm compression), ophthalmoplegia, hypopituitarism — EMERGENCY
CT: Hyperdense within sella/adenoma (acute haemorrhage)
MRI: T1 bright (subacute haemorrhage/met-Hb), T2 variable. Look for chiasmal displacement, cavernous sinus involvement.
Treatment: Urgent decompression if visual compromise; steroids for hypopituitarism
→ **Microadenoma vs Macroadenoma:**
Micro: <10mm, usually intrasellar, may not deform contour. Best seen on dynamic gadolinium (early non-enhancement — adenoma enhances late relative to normal pituitary)
Macro: >10mm, suprasellar extension, chiasm/cavernous sinus involvement
→ **Cavernous sinus invasion grading (Knosp):**
Grade 0–1: Medial to carotid line (not invasive)
Grade 2: To or touching carotid line
Grade 3–4: Lateral to carotid line (invasive, surgical challenge)
→ **Rathke Cleft Cyst (RCC):**
Intrasellar/suprasellar cyst. T2 bright (serous) or T1 bright (mucinous/proteinaceous). No enhancement (wall may enhance minimally). Intracystic nodule is characteristic.
Differentiates from craniopharyngioma: No calcification, no solid enhancing component.
→ **Incidentaloma management principle:** Microadenoma <6mm without visual/endocrine symptoms — MRI in 1 year, then 2 years, then stop if stable. Any visual symptom → formal field testing.
→ **India context:** Prolactinoma commonest adenoma (galactorrhoea + amenorrhoea in young women). Bromocriptine responsive.

title: Spinal Vascular Lesions — Dural AVF vs AVM vs Cavernoma
body:
→ **Spinal Dural AVF (most common spinal vascular malformation):**
Pathophysiology: Arteriovenous shunt at dural root sleeve → venous hypertension → cord oedema
Clinical: Middle-aged/elderly men, slowly progressive myelopathy, worse with exertion
MRI: Long segment central cord T2 hyperintensity (conus + lower thoracic), perimedullary flow voids (dilated draining veins on dorsal cord surface) = key finding
See also: Tram-track enhancement (existing entry), diffuse cord enhancement possible
Treatment: Endovascular or surgical disconnection
→ **Spinal AVM (Intramedullary or perimedullary):**
Age: Young patients, acute presentation (haemorrhage, SAH)
MRI: Intramedullary or perimedullary nidus, flow voids, haemosiderin (T2* dark), cord signal change from haemorrhage or ischaemia
Angiography: Definitive — nidus, feeding arteries, draining veins
Differentiates from dAVF: Age (young), haemorrhage, intramedullary location
→ **Spinal Cavernoma:**
Intramedullary, popcorn pattern (haemosiderin rim), T2* blooming
No flow voids (no high-flow component)
Presents with acute/subacute myelopathy (haemorrhage)
MRI: Heterogeneous T1/T2 core, T2* dark rim. No enhancement or minimal.
→ **Key differentiator:**
Dural AVF: Old man, slow, dorsal flow voids, no haemorrhage
AVM: Young, acute, intramedullary nidus, haemorrhage
Cavernoma: Popcorn, T2* blooming, no flow voids
→ **India context:** All three cause progressive myelopathy — MRI with GRE/SWI + gadolinium + spinal angiography sequence is critical workup

title: Dementia — MRI Pattern-Based Approach
body:
→ **Pattern-based approach to dementia imaging:**
→ **Hippocampal/medial temporal atrophy predominant:**
Alzheimer disease (AD): Bilateral medial temporal + posterior parietal + precuneus atrophy. Entorhinal cortex earliest. Medial temporal lobe atrophy score (MTA score ≥2 = significant)
→ **Frontotemporal atrophy predominant:**
Frontotemporal dementia (FTD): Asymmetric frontal/temporal atrophy. Knife blade atrophy of superior temporal sulcus (see existing entry). Spares hippocampus relatively early. Three variants: bvFTD, semantic, non-fluent.
→ **Basal ganglia + WM + symmetric pattern:**
Vascular dementia: Periventricular/subcortical WM disease, lacunar infarcts, strategic infarcts (thalamus, angular gyrus, basal forebrain)
DLB (Dementia with Lewy bodies): Relative preservation of medial temporal lobes (distinguishes from AD), posterior cortical atrophy, occipital hypometabolism on FDG-PET
PSP: Midbrain atrophy (hummingbird + Mickey Mouse signs — existing entries)
→ **Rapidly progressive dementia:**
CJD: Cortical ribbon sign + DWI restriction in cortex + striatum/thalamus. (existing entries)
Prion disease: Must always be in differential of rapidly progressive dementia
→ **Key MRI sequences:** 3D T1 (volumetry), T2/FLAIR (WM disease), DWI (CJD, infarcts), SWI (microbleeds — CAA, DLB)
→ **Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy (CAA):** Lobar microbleeds + macrobleed (SWI), cortical superficial siderosis, elderly. Risk factor for lobar ICH.
→ **India context:** Vascular dementia more prevalent (hypertension burden). TB meningitis as a reversible cause of cognitive decline must always be excluded.

title: Spinal Tuberculosis (Pott Disease) — Imaging Features
body:
→ **Pott disease = most common cause of spinal cord compression in India**
→ **Classic features:**
Disc involvement (DISC SPACE NARROWING is characteristic — malignancy spares disc!)
Adjoining vertebral body involvement (two contiguous vertebrae most common)
Paraspinal cold abscess (subligamentous spread under ALL/PLL — extends multiple levels)
T1 dark, T2 bright vertebral body signal (marrow replacement)
Gibbus deformity (kyphosis from vertebral collapse)
→ **MRI signal of abscess:**
T2 bright, rim enhancement (cold abscess wall enhances uniformly)
Psoas abscess: Bilateral, extends along psoas, may calcify in chronic stage
→ **Skip lesions:** Non-contiguous vertebral involvement — rare but occurs in TB (important to image whole spine)
→ **TB vs Pyogenic spondylodiscitis:**
TB: Slow, disc preserved early, large cold abscess, subligamentous spread, posterior element involvement possible
Pyogenic: Rapid, disc destroyed early, smaller abscess, Staphylococcus aureus (IV drug users, post-procedure)
→ **TB vs Malignancy (metastasis):**
TB: Disc involved, paraspinal abscess, younger patient, no known primary
Malignancy: Disc SPARED, posterior elements (pedicles) involved, known primary
→ **Spinal canal compromise:** Always assess thecal sac compression, cord signal change (myelopathy)
→ **Neurological deficit + paraspinal abscess + vertebral destruction = Pott disease emergency**
→ **India context:** Thoracolumbar junction most common. Lumbar > thoracic > cervical. Coexistent pulmonary TB ~50%.

title: Brain Abscess — Stages and Mimics
body:
→ **Stages of pyogenic brain abscess:**
1. **Early cerebritis (days 1–3):** Ill-defined T2 hyperintensity, no capsule, poorly defined enhancement or none. DWI may be positive centrally.
2. **Late cerebritis (days 4–9):** More defined area, early capsule formation, ring enhancement develops
3. **Early capsule (days 10–13):** Well-defined ring enhancement, T2 dark capsule (fibrous/haemosiderin), central DWI restriction
4. **Late capsule (>2 weeks):** Complete ring, capsule thinner on ventricular side (less vascular) — predisposes to intraventricular rupture
→ **MRI hallmarks of established abscess:**
DWI restricts (ADC low) = viscous pus (key differentiator from metastasis/GBM which do NOT restrict centrally)
Dual rim sign: Inner T2 dark rim (free radical/paramagnetic) + outer T2 bright rim (oedema)
MRS: Amino acid peaks (valine, leucine, isoleucine) at 0.9ppm + acetate + succinate = pyogenic
→ **India context — organisms:**
Community: Streptococcus (dental, ENT source), anaerobes, mixed
Immunocompromised: Toxoplasma (see HIV pearl), fungal (aspergillus, cryptococcus)
Post-traumatic/surgical: Staphylococcus aureus
Cysticercosis stage 2 = colloidal cyst (DWI does NOT restrict — differentiates from pyogenic abscess)
→ **Fungal abscess:** May not restrict on DWI. Rim shows T2 dark internal projections (hyphae) = characteristic of aspergillus. Affects skull base, sinus extension.
→ **Ventricular rupture:** Pyogenic abscess ruptures into ventricle → ventriculitis (ependymal DWI restriction, enhancement, debris in ventricles) = high mortality

title: Spinal Cord Demyelination — MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD
body:
→ **This pearl focuses on the SPINAL CORD features; brain distribution is in existing entry #17**
→ **MS spinal cord:**
Short segment: <2 vertebral levels (usually 1 VL)
Peripheral location: Posterior or lateral columns (dorsal/lateral)
Cross-sectional: <50% cord cross-section (partial, peripheral)
Cervical predominance
No cord expansion (or minimal)
Enhancement only in acute phase (<4–6 weeks)
Chronic: Cord atrophy, T2 bright foci remain
→ **NMOSD (AQP4-IgG) spinal cord:**
Long segment: ≥3 vertebral levels (longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis — LETM)
Central grey matter predominance (H-shaped on axial = existing sign entry)
Expansion of cord in acute phase
Bright spotty lesion on axial T2 (central grey)
Area postrema lesion in brainstem (intractable hiccoughs/vomiting)
Cervicothoracic or thoracic, may extend to conus
→ **MOGAD (MOG-IgG) spinal cord:**
LECM or short, central predominance also possible
Conus often involved (more than NMOSD)
Enhancement prominent, leptomeningeal enhancement possible
T2 bright, may involve entire cord cross-section
Brain lesions: Bilateral optic neuritis, ADEM-like cortical lesions
→ **Key differentiator table:**
Short + peripheral + cervical = MS
Long + central grey + area postrema = NMOSD
Long + conus + bilateral ON + ADEM pattern = MOGAD
→ **Clinical pearl:** Seronegative LETM — consider MOGAD and do MOG-IgG before labelling as seronegative NMOSD. Treatment differs significantly.
